history_blurb: Haring Street in Gravesend is named for the Haring family, Dutch colonial settlers who owned farmland in the area. The family name appears in records going back to the 17th century, when southern Brooklyn was a patchwork of Dutch farming communities.

fact_text: Harman Street was originally named for the Harman family, early landowners in the Bushwick area during its Dutch farming days.
history_blurb: Harman Street runs through Bushwick and is named for the Harman family, early landowners in the area. Bushwick was originally settled by the Dutch in 1661 as one of the original six towns of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Harrison Avenue honors Benjamin Harrison V of Virginia, a Declaration of Independence signer, one of 43 signers with Brooklyn streets.
history_blurb: Harrison Avenue is one of over 40 streets in South Williamsburg and Bed-Stuy named after Declaration of Independence signers around 1846. Benjamin Harrison V (1726 to 1791) was a Virginia planter who signed the Declaration and served as governor of Virginia.

fact_text: Hart Street honors John Hart of New Jersey, a Declaration signer whose farm was destroyed by the British in retaliation.
history_blurb: Hart Street in Bed-Stuy is named for John Hart (c. 1711 to 1779), a New Jersey farmer and signer of the Declaration of Independence. It is part of a large group of Brooklyn streets named for Declaration signers. The street is also home to the recently designated Willoughby-Hart Historic District.

fact_text: Harway Avenue is named for developer James Harway, but in the 1930s it swapped names with Cropsey Avenue in one of Brooklyn's oddest cartographic mysteries.
history_blurb: Harway Avenue in Bath Beach is named for 19th century developer James Harway. In one of Brooklyn's odder cartographic mysteries, Harway and Cropsey Avenues swapped names in the 1930s, likely during a WPA road widening project. The original Harway Avenue is now called Cropsey.

fact_text: Hastings Street in East New York was part of the 1887 street renaming, likely named for the English town or the Battle of Hastings.
history_blurb: Hastings Street was named during the 1887 New Lots Board of Trade reorganization of street names. The name references Hastings in East Sussex, England, famous for the 1066 battle where William the Conqueror defeated King Harold II and changed the course of English history.

fact_text: Hattie Jones Place honors a beloved local community leader, one of Brooklyn's many streets renamed for neighborhood activists.
history_blurb: Hattie Jones Place is a jointly named street honoring a respected community figure in the neighborhood. Brooklyn has a tradition of renaming or joint naming streets for local leaders, educators, and activists who made a lasting impact on their communities.

fact_text: Hawthorne Street honors Nathaniel Hawthorne, author of 'The Scarlet Letter', part of Flatbush's grid of literary street names.
history_blurb: Hawthorne Street in Prospect Lefferts Gardens is named for Nathaniel Hawthorne (1804 to 1864), the novelist who wrote 'The Scarlet Letter' and 'The House of the Seven Gables.' The street sits in a Flatbush neighborhood where 19th century developers favored literary and historical names to attract buyers.

fact_text: Hegeman Avenue honors the Hegeman family, prominent Dutch settlers and landowners in the Flatlands area of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Hegeman Avenue in Brownsville and East New York is named for the Hegeman family, Dutch colonial settlers who owned significant tracts of farmland in Flatlands. The family name appears throughout Brooklyn's early land records and church rolls dating to the 1600s.

fact_text: Hemlock Street in East New York is named for the hemlock tree, part of a cluster of Brooklyn streets bearing tree names.
history_blurb: Hemlock Street runs through East New York and Cypress Hills. It belongs to a group of Brooklyn streets named for trees, including nearby Holly, Cypress, and Pine. The naming convention was common when developers laid out residential grids in formerly wooded areas.

fact_text: Hendrickson Street honors the Hendrickson family, Dutch settlers who were among the earliest landowners in southern Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Hendrickson Street in Canarsie is named for the Hendrickson family, early Dutch colonial settlers who farmed in the Canarsie and Flatlands areas. The family name appears in land records from the 1600s and several family members served as local officials.

fact_text: Hendrix Street was named for the Hendrix family, early property owners in the East New York area, not the famous guitarist.
history_blurb: Hendrix Street in East New York is named for the Hendrix family, local landowners during the neighborhood's 19th century development. Despite what some assume, the street long predates rock legend Jimi Hendrix (1942 to 1970).

fact_text: Henry Street is named for Dr. Thomas W. Henry, a prominent 1820s physician, not Patrick Henry as many assume.
history_blurb: Henry Street in Brooklyn Heights honors Dr. Thomas W. Henry, president of the Kings County Medical Society in the 1820s. This is distinct from Manhattan's Henry Street, which is named for Henry Rutgers. The duplication reflects Brooklyn and Manhattan's separate development before the 1898 consolidation.

fact_text: The street was named during the neighborhood's mid 19th century development. Greenpoint's streets were largely laid out when the area transitioned from farmland to an industrial hub known for shipbuilding and glass manufacturing.
history_blurb: Herbert Street runs through Greenpoint. The street was named during the neighborhood's mid 19th century development. Greenpoint's streets were largely laid out when the area transitioned from farmland to an industrial hub known for shipbuilding and glass manufacturing.

fact_text: Herkimer Street honors Nicholas Herkimer, the general who was mortally wounded leading the militia at the Battle of Oriskany in 1777.
history_blurb: Herkimer Street in Bedford-Stuyvesant is named for Brigadier General Nicholas Herkimer (1728 to 1777), who led the Tryon County militia at the Battle of Oriskany during the Revolutionary War. Wounded in the leg, he propped himself against a tree and continued directing the battle while smoking his pipe.

fact_text: Herzl Street was renamed from Ames Street in 1913 to honor the father of Zionism, reflecting Brownsville's 'Little Jerusalem' era.
history_blurb: In 1913, residents of heavily Jewish Brownsville petitioned to rename Ames Street after Theodor Herzl (1860 to 1904), the journalist who founded modern Zionism. At the time, over 80,000 of Brownsville's 100,000 residents were Russian Jews, earning the neighborhood the nickname 'Little Jerusalem.'

fact_text: Heyward Street honors Thomas Heyward Jr., a signer of the Declaration of Independence from South Carolina.
history_blurb: Heyward Street in Williamsburg is named for Thomas Heyward Jr. (1746 to 1809), a South Carolina planter and signer of the Declaration of Independence. He was captured by the British during the Siege of Charleston in 1780 and held prisoner in St. Augustine, Florida, for a year.

fact_text: Hicks Street is named for the Hicks brothers, John and Jacob, who operated Brooklyn's only ferry to Manhattan in the early 1800s.
history_blurb: Hicks Street in Brooklyn Heights honors brothers John and Jacob Middagh Hicks, early 18th century landowners who ran the Fulton Ferry across the East River. Their mother's maiden name, Middagh, is preserved in nearby Middagh Street. The brothers sold off their Heights property to tradesmen and artisans.

fact_text: Highland Avenue takes its name from the elevated terrain of Highland Park, which sits on the glacial ridge between Brooklyn and Queens.
history_blurb: Highland Avenue runs along the border of Brooklyn and Queens near Highland Park. The park sits atop a glacial moraine, the terminal ridge left by the last Ice Age glacier. The Ridgewood Reservoir, built in 1858 to supply Brooklyn's drinking water, sits within the park.

fact_text: Highland Boulevard follows the high ridge near Highland Park, the glacial moraine that forms Brooklyn's natural spine.
history_blurb: Highland Boulevard runs near Highland Park along the glacial ridge separating Brooklyn from Queens. The boulevard's elevated position offers views that once made it a desirable address. Highland Park itself contains the historic Ridgewood Reservoir.

fact_text: Hill Street in Greenpoint is named for the slight rise in elevation at its location. While Brooklyn is often thought of as flat, the borough has several notable hills, particularly along the glacial moraine that runs through its center.
history_blurb: Hill Street in Greenpoint is named for the slight rise in elevation at its location. While Brooklyn is often thought of as flat, the borough has several notable hills, particularly along the glacial moraine that runs through its center.

fact_text: Hillel Place was once called Germania Place, renamed in 1959 for the ancient Jewish sage when Brooklyn College built its Hillel center.
history_blurb: Hillel Place near Brooklyn College was originally Germania Place, named for the Germania Real Estate Company that developed the area in the 1890s. It was renamed around 1959 when the college's Hillel building opened, honoring the first century Jewish sage famous for asking 'If I am not for myself, who is for me?'

fact_text: Himrod Street is named for Rev. J.S. Himrod, the first pastor of the South Bushwick Reformed Dutch Church founded in 1853.
history_blurb: Himrod Street in Bushwick honors Reverend J.S. Himrod, the founding pastor of the South Bushwick Reformed Dutch Church. The church at Bushwick Avenue and Himrod Street dates to 1853, reflecting the neighborhood's deep Dutch Reformed roots.

fact_text: Hinsdale Street in East New York was named during the 1887 renaming, likely for a local landowner or an English place name.
history_blurb: Hinsdale Street was named during the 1887 New Lots Board of Trade reorganization that renamed 67 streets in the East New York area. The name may reference a local property owner or an English place name.

fact_text: Holly Street runs through East New York and is part of a group of streets named for trees. Nearby Hemlock, Pine, Cypress, and Chestnut streets follow the same botanical naming pattern, common in areas developed from formerly wooded land.
history_blurb: Holly Street runs through East New York and is part of a group of streets named for trees. Nearby Hemlock, Pine, Cypress, and Chestnut streets follow the same botanical naming pattern, common in areas developed from formerly wooded land.

fact_text: Homecrest Avenue takes its name from a 1900 planned suburb marketed as 'the ideal home place' by developer T.B. Ackerson.
history_blurb: Homecrest was branded in 1900 by developer T.B. Ackerson as a middle class garden suburb in southern Brooklyn. 'Home' emphasized domestic comfort and 'Crest' evoked the area's slight elevation above the surrounding coastal flats of old Gravesend.

fact_text: Hooper Street honors William Hooper, a Declaration signer. It was originally just Eleventh Street in Williamsburg's numbered grid.
history_blurb: Hooper Street was originally Eleventh Street in Williamsburg's numbered grid. When streets were renamed in 1886 to avoid confusion with other Brooklyn numbered streets, it was rechristened for William Hooper, a Declaration of Independence signer from North Carolina.

fact_text: Hope Street was formerly North Street, renamed in 1885 when Williamsburg ditched its confusing numbered grid.
history_blurb: Hope Street was originally North Street in the old Williamsburg numbered grid. In 1885 the numbered and directional streets were renamed to avoid confusion with Brooklyn's other grids. The abstract virtue name 'Hope' replaced the directional designation.

fact_text: Hopkins Street honors Stephen Hopkins, the Rhode Island governor who signed the Declaration, one of 43 signers with Brooklyn streets.
history_blurb: Hopkins Street was mapped around 1846 as part of an initiative that gave 43 of the 56 Declaration of Independence signers streets in South Williamsburg and Bed-Stuy. Stephen Hopkins served as governor of Rhode Island and signed both the Continental Association and the Declaration.

fact_text: Howard Avenue honors William Howard, whose Rising Sun Tavern stood nearby. The British forced him to guide troops during the Battle of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Howard Avenue was laid out in 1839 and named for William Howard (1725 to 1777), who ran the Rising Sun Tavern at the Jamaica and Cripplebush roads. During the Battle of Brooklyn, British General Howe forced Howard and his son to guide troops along the Rockaway Path.

fact_text: Hudson Avenue honors Henry Hudson, the English explorer whose 1609 voyage up the river that bears his name opened New York to Dutch colonization.
history_blurb: Hudson Avenue in Downtown Brooklyn and Vinegar Hill is named for Henry Hudson (c. 1565 to 1611), the English explorer who sailed the Half Moon into New York Harbor in 1609 while searching for a Northwest Passage. His voyage led to Dutch colonization and the founding of New Amsterdam.

fact_text: Hull Street in Bedford-Stuyvesant was named for a local family. It sits in one of Brooklyn's most architecturally significant neighborhoods.
history_blurb: Hull Street runs through Bedford-Stuyvesant. It was named for the Hull family, local property owners. The street features many well preserved 19th century brownstones and row houses that make Bed-Stuy one of New York City's most architecturally rich neighborhoods.

fact_text: Hunterfly Place preserves the Dutch name 'Ander Vly' (to the swampy place), and three 1840s houses from the free Black community of Weeksville.
namesake: Ander Vly (Dutch: to the swampy place)
history_blurb: Hunterfly Place is a remnant of Hunterfly Road, originally a Native American trail the Dutch called 'Ander Vly' (to the low or swampy place). The road ran through Weeksville, one of America's first free Black communities, founded by James Weeks in 1838. Three surviving 1840s houses are now the Weeksville Heritage Center.

fact_text: Huntington Street in Carroll Gardens may honor Samuel Huntington, a signer of the Declaration of Independence and President of the Continental Congress.
history_blurb: Huntington Street runs through Carroll Gardens and Red Hook. It is likely named for Samuel Huntington (1731 to 1796), a signer of the Declaration of Independence who served as President of the Continental Congress from 1779 to 1781, making him technically the first president of the united states under the Articles of Confederation.

fact_text: Huron Street was originally just H Street, likely renamed for the locally built steamship USS Huron in the 1850s.
history_blurb: Greenpoint's streets were laid out alphabetically and Huron was originally H Street. When full names replaced letters in the 1850s, H Street became Huron, probably after a steamship built in the neighborhood's famous shipyards. The name fits Greenpoint's maritime heritage alongside India and Java Streets.

fact_text: Imlay Street was renamed in 1861 for William H. Imlay, who both sold land to the Atlantic Dock Company and held major shares in it.
history_blurb: Imlay Street was originally Hudson Street but was renamed in 1861 for William H. Imlay, who both sold land to the Atlantic Dock Company and held major shares in it. The street runs through Red Hook, Brooklyn's historic waterfront district.

fact_text: Independence Avenue in Bay Ridge celebrates American independence, part of a patriotic naming cluster in the southwestern Brooklyn neighborhood.
history_blurb: Independence Avenue runs through the Bay Ridge section of Brooklyn. The patriotic name reflects a common 19th century practice of naming streets for American ideals. Bay Ridge and nearby Fort Hamilton have several streets with military and patriotic themes.

fact_text: Ingraham Street likely honors Duncan Ingraham, the Navy officer who won a Congressional Gold Medal for rescuing a political refugee in 1853.
history_blurb: Ingraham Street is likely named for Duncan Nathaniel Ingraham (1802 to 1891), a U.S. Navy officer celebrated for his 1853 intervention at Smyrna to free Martin Koszta, a Hungarian refugee, from Austrian detention. Congress awarded him thanks and a Gold Medal for the daring rescue.

fact_text: Irving Avenue honors Washington Irving, the author who gave America its first international literary hit with 'Rip Van Winkle.'
history_blurb: Irving Avenue in Bushwick is named for Washington Irving (1783 to 1859), the author of 'Rip Van Winkle' and 'The Legend of Sleepy Hollow.' Irving was the first American writer to earn a living solely through his pen and the first to gain international acclaim. He also coined the term 'Gotham' for New York City.

fact_text: Irving Place also honors Washington Irving, the writer who coined 'Gotham' as a nickname for New York City.
history_blurb: Irving Place, like Irving Avenue, is named for Washington Irving (1783 to 1859). Beyond his famous short stories, Irving served as U.S. Ambassador to Spain and wrote an acclaimed biography of George Washington. His Sunnyside estate on the Hudson is now a historic landmark.

fact_text: Irvington Place likely takes its name from Irvington, the Hudson Valley village named for author Washington Irving.
history_blurb: Irvington Place in Brooklyn may be named for the village of Irvington on Hudson, which was itself named in honor of Washington Irving. The author lived near the village at his estate Sunnyside, and the community changed its name from Dearman to Irvington in his honor in 1854.

fact_text: Jackie Robinson Parkway was renamed in 1997 from the Interboro Parkway to honor the baseball legend who broke the color barrier with the Brooklyn Dodgers.
history_blurb: Jackie Robinson Parkway was originally the Interboro Parkway, built in the 1930s. It was renamed in 1997 for Jackie Robinson (1919 to 1972), who broke Major League Baseball's color barrier when he joined the Brooklyn Dodgers on April 15, 1947. The parkway connects Brooklyn to Queens near Highland Park.

fact_text: Jamaica Avenue follows an ancient Lenape trail. Its name comes from 'Jameco,' a Native American word possibly meaning 'beaver.'
history_blurb: Jamaica Avenue follows a path that was originally a Lenape trail connecting Brooklyn to Long Island. The name derives from 'Jameco' (also written as Yamecah), a Native American word that may mean 'beaver' or 'busy one.' The avenue became a major commercial corridor spanning Brooklyn and Queens.

fact_text: Jefferson Avenue honors Thomas Jefferson, the principal author of the Declaration of Independence and America's third president.
history_blurb: Jefferson Avenue in Bedford-Stuyvesant is named for Thomas Jefferson (1743 to 1826), the third President of the United States and principal author of the Declaration of Independence. The street sits in a neighborhood where many streets honor Founding Fathers and Revolutionary War figures.

fact_text: Jerome Avenue is named for Leonard Jerome, Wall Street's 'King' and investor in the Sheepshead Bay Race Track, also Winston Churchill's grandfather.
history_blurb: Jerome Avenue is named for Leonard Jerome (1817 to 1891), a wealthy financier nicknamed the King of Wall Street. He was a principal investor in the Sheepshead Bay Race Track, and the street bordered the racetrack grounds. Jerome's daughter Jennie married Lord Randolph Churchill, making Jerome Winston Churchill's grandfather.

fact_text: Jerome Street in East New York was likely named for the same Jerome family, prominent in New York society and real estate.
history_blurb: Jerome Street in East New York likely shares its namesake with Jerome Avenue. Leonard Jerome was one of the wealthiest men in Gilded Age New York and a major investor in real estate and horse racing.

fact_text: Jewel Street in Greenpoint belongs to a cluster of streets named for precious materials, Gem, Diamond, and Ruby streets are nearby.
history_blurb: Jewel Street in Greenpoint is part of a group of streets named for precious stones and materials. Nearby streets include Gem, Diamond, Ruby, and Sapphire. The naming pattern was common in 19th century developments and reflects the era's taste for aspirational street names.

fact_text: Named for John Haberdinck, a Dutch shoemaker who owned the Shoemakers Field the street crossed.
history_blurb: John Street honors John Haberdinck, a wealthy Dutch shoemaker who owned land here and bequeathed his 35 acre Shoemakers Field to the Collegiate Dutch Church. Part of the street was also once called Golden Hill, after a nearby wheat field.

fact_text: Johnson Avenue honors Jeremiah Johnson, Brooklyn's third mayor who served for 40 years as town supervisor.
history_blurb: Johnson Avenue honors Jeremiah Johnson (1766 to 1852), Brooklyn's third mayor who served 1837 to 1838. He was also Brooklyn's town supervisor for 40 years and a noted local historian. He fiercely opposed Brooklyn's consolidation with New York City.

fact_text: Johnson Street honors the Johnson family estate, Rev. Evan Johnson developed it as an upper middle class enclave in the 1830s.
history_blurb: Johnson Street was named for the Johnson family estate. Rev. Evan Johnson inherited his wife's share of the property and developed it as an upper middle class enclave in the late 1820s 1840s. His brother Samuel Johnson built at least eight houses on the street by 1850.

fact_text: Joralemon Street honors Teunis Joralemon, the farmer whose orchard covered the land where this Brooklyn Heights street now runs.
history_blurb: Joralemon Street in Brooklyn Heights is named for Teunis Joralemon, who owned a farm and orchard on the site in the early 1800s. The street is also notable for housing a secret emergency exit for the 4/5 subway line, a ventilation building at 58 Joralemon Street disguised as a brownstone.

fact_text: Judge Street in Williamsburg is a small street whose namesake remains uncertain, possibly named for a local magistrate.
history_blurb: Judge Street is a short street in Williamsburg. The origin of the name is uncertain but may reference a local judge or magistrate from the neighborhood's early development period.

fact_text: Kane Street in Carroll Gardens honors John K. Kane, a Brooklyn civic leader. It's also home to the oldest continuously operating synagogue in Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Kane Street in Carroll Gardens and Cobble Hill is named for John K. Kane, a civic figure in 19th century Brooklyn. The street is home to Congregation Beth Israel (the Kane Street Synagogue), founded in 1856 and the oldest continuously operating synagogue in Brooklyn.

fact_text: Harbor View Terrace's name reflects its elevated views of New York Harbor and the Narrows. It gained pop culture fame as the exterior of the Reagan family home on TV's 'Blue Bloods.'
history_blurb: Harbor View Terrace is a secluded street in Bay Ridge with 1920s neo Tudor and neo Colonial homes. The name reflects its elevated views of New York Harbor and the Narrows. It gained pop culture fame as the exterior of the Reagan family home on TV's 'Blue Bloods.'

fact_text: Hendrickson Place, like nearby Hendrickson Street, is named for the Hendrickson family, early Dutch settlers in the Canarsie and Flatlands areas of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Hendrickson Place, like nearby Hendrickson Street, is named for the Hendrickson family, early Dutch settlers in the Canarsie and Flatlands areas of Brooklyn. The family were prominent landowners whose name appears throughout colonial era records.

fact_text: Herkimer Place, like Herkimer Street, is named for Brigadier General Nicholas Herkimer (1728 to 1777), who led American forces at the Battle of Oriskany.
history_blurb: Herkimer Place, like Herkimer Street, is named for Brigadier General Nicholas Herkimer (1728 to 1777), who led American forces at the Battle of Oriskany. Despite being mortally wounded, he continued to command his troops while propped against a tree.

fact_text: Highland Place takes its name from the elevated terrain near Highland Park on Brooklyn's eastern ridge.
history_blurb: Highland Place sits near Highland Park, named for its position on the glacial moraine that forms a natural ridge through Brooklyn. The park's hilltop location offered commanding views and made it a strategic point in the area's development.

fact_text: Hubbard Place honors James Hubbard, one of the original 1645 patentees of Gravesend. He followed Lady Deborah Moody to Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Hubbard Place is named for the family of James Hubbard, who in 1645 was one of the original patentees of the town of Gravesend, founded by Lady Deborah Moody. Hubbard followed Moody from Salem after being punished for his religious beliefs. The nearby Hubbard House at 2138 McDonald Avenue is a landmark farmhouse from around 1830.

fact_text: Hubbard Street honors the same James Hubbard, a 1645 Gravesend patentee who fled Salem for religious freedom.
history_blurb: Hubbard Street carries the same namesake as Hubbard Place: James Hubbard, one of the original patentees of Gravesend in 1645. He was among a group of religious dissenters who followed Lady Deborah Moody from New England to establish a community based on religious tolerance in southern Brooklyn.

fact_text: Hunter Avenue in East New York was named for a local property owner, part of the grid laid out during the neighborhood's 19th century growth.
history_blurb: Hunter Avenue runs through East New York and was named for the Hunter family, local landowners. The street was laid out during the neighborhood's expansion, when the area transitioned from farmland to residential development.

fact_text: Hammerhead Avenue inside Brooklyn Navy Yard is named for the massive 350 ton crane that once towered 25 stories over the shipyard.
history_blurb: Hammerhead Avenue commemorates the iconic 350 ton hammerhead crane installed at Pier G in 1943. The crane placed turrets and gun barrels on warships during WWII. It was dismantled in 1965 when yard operations wound down. The Navy Yard's streets are privately maintained.

fact_text: Hart Place takes its name from Hart Street, which honors John Hart, one of 43 Declaration signers given Brooklyn streets around 1846.
history_blurb: Hart Place derives from Hart Street, named for John Hart of New Jersey, one of 56 signers of the Declaration of Independence. Around 1846, 43 Declaration signers had streets mapped and named for them in South Williamsburg and Bed-Stuy.

fact_text: Henderson Walk in Coney Island is named for Henry Henderson's Bathing Pavilion, once the largest on the island.
history_blurb: Henderson Walk took its name from Henry Henderson's Bathing Pavilion, the largest in Coney Island. The pavilion was destroyed in a fire on May 26, 1899, but the street preserved its name.

fact_text: The court takes its name from adjacent Herkimer Street.
history_blurb: Herkimer Court is a small court of humble two family worker homes in Bed-Stuy, built in 1884 by developer E.R. Betts. The first three houses were designed by D.H. Fowler and the remaining seven by prolific local architect Amzi Hill. The court takes its name from adjacent Herkimer Street.

fact_text: Homecrest Court takes its name from the Homecrest neighborhood, a turn of the century planned suburban community by developer T.B. Ackerson.
history_blurb: Homecrest Court is named for the surrounding Homecrest neighborhood. Developers coined the name around 1900 to market one of southern Brooklyn's first planned suburbs on old Gravesend farmland.

fact_text: Hendrix Place in East New York is named for the Ephraim Hendricks family, early Dutch settlers in the area.
history_blurb: Hendrix Place in East New York is named after the Ephraim Hendricks family, part of the Dutch colonial heritage of Brooklyn. The street sits in an area originally developed by John Pitkin in the 1830s on land purchased from descendants of Dutch settlers.

fact_text: Like many Hudson named streets in New York, it references Henry Hudson, the English explorer whose 1609 voyage up the river that now bears his name opened the region to Dutch colonization.
history_blurb: Hudson Walk is a small street in the Downtown Brooklyn area. Like many Hudson named streets in New York, it references Henry Hudson, the English explorer whose 1609 voyage up the river that now bears his name opened the region to Dutch colonization.

fact_text: Howard Place in Windsor Terrace is named for William Howard, a partner in the Brooklyn brewing firm Howard and Fuller.
history_blurb: Howard Place first appears on maps in the 1880s near Prospect Park. It is named for William Howard, a partner in the Brooklyn brewing concern Howard and Fuller.

fact_text: Jackson Street was probably named for Daniel Jackson, a local property owner with landed interests in early Williamsburgh.
history_blurb: Jackson Street was likely named for Daniel Jackson, who had landed interests in early Williamsburgh. The street originally ran from Front Street to Newtown Creek but part of it was later renamed Charlick Street.

fact_text: Irwin Street is part of Manhattan Beach's alphabetical A to P grid of English themed street names laid out in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Irwin Street is one of 16 alphabetically ordered streets in Manhattan Beach named for English places and figures. The grid was laid out in the 1870s 1880s when developer Austin Corbin built the neighborhood as an exclusive seaside resort.

fact_text: Jaffray Street is the 'J' in Manhattan Beach's A to P alphabetical grid of English themed street names.
history_blurb: Jaffray Street sits between Irwin and Kensington in Manhattan Beach's alphabetically ordered street system. The 16 streets were named for English places and figures when developer Austin Corbin laid out the seaside resort neighborhood in the late 1870s.

fact_text: Karweg Place in Gravesend is named for the Karweg family, early Dutch settlers who farmed the area.
history_blurb: Karweg Place in Gravesend is named for the Karweg family, early Dutch settlers who were among the landowners in the area. The short street sits in a neighborhood originally settled as one of the six original towns of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Kenilworth Place takes its name from Kenilworth Castle in England, part of Flatbush's tradition of borrowing English place names.
history_blurb: Kenilworth Place in Flatbush is named for Kenilworth Castle, a medieval fortress in Warwickshire, England. Many streets in Flatbush and surrounding neighborhoods were given English names during late 19th century development as a marketing tool for middle class homebuyers.

fact_text: Kenmore Terrace in Flatbush is named for Kenmore, the Fredericksburg plantation home of George Washington's sister.
history_blurb: Kenmore Terrace takes its name from Kenmore, the 18th century plantation in Fredericksburg, Virginia, that was home to Betty Washington Lewis, George Washington's sister. The name was fashionable in late 19th century American real estate developments.

fact_text: Kensington Street is named for the London borough, fitting for a Brooklyn neighborhood that borrowed its name from the same royal district.
history_blurb: Kensington Street sits in the Brooklyn neighborhood of Kensington, which took its name from the Royal Borough of Kensington in London. The area was developed in the late 1800s when English place names lent an air of refinement to new suburban communities.

fact_text: Kent Street in Greenpoint was originally K Street, renamed for James Kent, the influential New York jurist known as 'the American Blackstone.'
history_blurb: Kent Street was originally K Street in Greenpoint's alphabetical grid. When letters were replaced with full names, it was named for James Kent (1763 to 1847), Chancellor of New York and author of Commentaries on American Law, earning him the nickname 'the American Blackstone.'

fact_text: Kermit Place in Midwood is named for Kermit Roosevelt, Theodore Roosevelt's adventurous son who explored Brazil and fought in both World Wars.
history_blurb: Kermit Place is named for Kermit Roosevelt (1889 to 1943), the second son of President Theodore Roosevelt. He accompanied his father on the perilous 1913 to 14 River of Doubt expedition in Brazil and served in both World Wars. The street was likely named during the early 20th century development of Midwood.

fact_text: Kimball Street is part of Manhattan Beach's alphabetical grid of streets laid out in the 1870s by developer Austin Corbin.
history_blurb: Kimball Street is the 'K' in Manhattan Beach's A to P alphabetical grid of street names. The 16 streets were laid out when developer Austin Corbin built the neighborhood as an exclusive seaside resort in the late 1870s.

fact_text: Despite the American Revolution, many such royally named streets kept their names. The street runs through Clinton Hill near the Brooklyn Navy Yard.
history_blurb: King Street in Brooklyn dates to the colonial era when naming streets for the crown was a common act of loyalty. Despite the American Revolution, many such royally named streets kept their names. The street runs through Clinton Hill near the Brooklyn Navy Yard.

fact_text: Knapp Street in Sheepshead Bay honors Shepherd Knapp, a 19th century NYC banker and philanthropist who served as president of the Mechanics' Bank.
history_blurb: Knapp Street is named for Shepherd Knapp (1795 to 1877), who served as president of the Mechanics' Bank of New York. He was also active in philanthropy and civic affairs. The street runs through the Marine Park and Sheepshead Bay neighborhoods of southern Brooklyn.

fact_text: Knickerbocker Avenue takes its name from Washington Irving's fictional historian Diedrich Knickerbocker, the character who gave all New Yorkers their nickname.
history_blurb: Knickerbocker Avenue is named for Diedrich Knickerbocker, the fictional Dutch historian created by Washington Irving in his 1809 satirical book A History of New-York. The character became so popular that 'Knickerbocker' became a nickname for all New Yorkers, especially those of Dutch descent.

fact_text: Korean War Veterans Plaza in Downtown Brooklyn was dedicated in 2002, one of the few NYC spaces honoring the 'Forgotten War.'
history_blurb: Korean War Veterans Plaza at Cadman Plaza in Downtown Brooklyn was dedicated in 2002 to honor the Americans who served in the Korean War (1950 to 1953). The conflict is often called the 'Forgotten War' because it fell between the heavily memorialized World War II and Vietnam War.

fact_text: Kossuth Place honors Lajos Kossuth, the Hungarian freedom fighter who drew massive NYC crowds during his 1851 American tour.
history_blurb: Kossuth Place is named for Lajos Kossuth (1802 to 1894), the Hungarian revolutionary leader who led the 1848 uprising against Austrian rule. He toured America in 1851 to 52 and received a hero's welcome in New York City, where enormous crowds greeted him.

fact_text: Kouwenhoven Lane preserves the name of one of Brooklyn's founding Dutch families. The Kouwenhovens settled New Utrecht in the 1650s.
history_blurb: Kouwenhoven Lane in southern Brooklyn is named for the Kouwenhoven family, Dutch settlers who were among the founders of New Utrecht in the 1650s. The family name appears in many variants (Conover, Couwenhoven) and their descendants were prominent landowners throughout southern Brooklyn for centuries.

fact_text: Lafayette Walk honors the Marquis de Lafayette, the French hero of the American Revolution who received a wild Brooklyn reception on his 1824 return visit.
history_blurb: Lafayette Walk is named for the Marquis de Lafayette (1757 to 1834), the French aristocrat who served as a major general in the Continental Army. When he returned to America in 1824, Brooklyn gave him a grand reception.

fact_text: Lake Street in Gravesend takes its name from the small freshwater lakes that once dotted the low lying marshlands of southern Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Lake Street in the Gravesend area references the small freshwater ponds and lakes that existed in the marshy terrain of southern Brooklyn before urban development. The area's low elevation and proximity to the coast created numerous small bodies of water.

fact_text: Lancaster Avenue in Brownsville may honor the Pennsylvania city, or the English House of Lancaster associated with the Wars of the Roses.
history_blurb: Lancaster Avenue in Brownsville is named either for the city of Lancaster, Pennsylvania, or for the English House of Lancaster. English place names were commonly used in Brooklyn street naming. The street runs through a neighborhood that saw rapid development in the late 19th century.

fact_text: Langham Street is the 'L' in Manhattan Beach's A to P alphabetical grid, named for the Langham Hotel, a grand London landmark.
history_blurb: Langham Street sits between Kimball and Mackenzie in Manhattan Beach's alphabetically ordered street system. It is likely named for the Langham Hotel in London, a grand Victorian era hotel that opened in 1865 and was one of Europe's largest.

fact_text: Laurel Avenue is one of several tree named streets in southern Brooklyn, a common 19th century naming convention for new developments.
history_blurb: Laurel Avenue joins a cluster of tree and plant named streets in southern Brooklyn, including Elm, Cedar, and Chestnut. This botanical naming pattern was popular in 19th century suburban developments, evoking a leafy, pastoral character.

fact_text: Lawrence Avenue likely honors Captain James Lawrence, the War of 1812 naval hero whose dying words were 'Don't give up the ship.'
history_blurb: Lawrence Avenue is likely named for Captain James Lawrence (1781 to 1813), the U.S. Navy officer famous for his dying command 'Don't give up the ship!' during the War of 1812 battle between the USS Chesapeake and HMS Shannon. His words became a rallying cry for the Navy.

fact_text: Lawrence Street in Downtown Brooklyn is named for Charles K. Lawrence, son in law of Dr. John Duffield, both men have streets steps apart.
history_blurb: Lawrence Street in downtown Brooklyn is named for Charles K. Lawrence, the son in law of Revolutionary War surgeon Dr. John Duffield. Gold, Duffield, Bridge, and Lawrence Streets between Fulton and Willoughby formed an early residential neighborhood developed in the 1840s.

fact_text: Williamsburg's streets honor Declaration signers, Lee Avenue is named for Virginia's Lee brothers, who helped launch American independence.
namesake: Richard Henry Lee & Francis Lightfoot Lee
history_blurb: Lee Avenue in Williamsburg was named around 1846 as part of a wave of street namings honoring signers of the Declaration of Independence. Richard Henry Lee introduced the 1776 resolution declaring the colonies free and independent states. His brother Francis Lightfoot Lee also signed the Declaration.

fact_text: Named for the Lefferts family, Dutch settlers who once owned most of what became Crown Heights and Bed-Stuy.
history_blurb: Lefferts Avenue honors the Lefferts family, descended from Leffert Pietersen Van Haughwout, who immigrated from Holland in 1660. The family became one of Brooklyn's wealthiest, owning vast tracts across Bedford-Stuyvesant and Crown Heights. Leffert Lefferts was the first president of the Long Island Bank, Brooklyn's first bank.

fact_text: Lefferts Place in Clinton Hill is named for the prominent Dutch Lefferts family, who owned most of modern day Bedford-Stuyvesant and Crown Heights North.
history_blurb: Lefferts Place in Clinton Hill is named for the prominent Dutch Lefferts family, who owned most of modern day Bedford-Stuyvesant and Crown Heights North. They farmed their large acreage with enslaved labor. Today, street and neighborhood names are nearly all that remain of their once vast influence.

fact_text: Lenox Road in Flatbush was likely named for James Lenox, the wealthy bibliophile whose collection helped found the New York Public Library.
history_blurb: Lenox Road likely takes its name from James Lenox (1800 to 1880), a prominent New York philanthropist and book collector. His personal library became a cornerstone of the New York Public Library when it merged with the Astor and Tilden foundations in 1895.

fact_text: This Bed-Stuy street echoes the Revolutionary War, named for the 1775 Battle of Lexington, the 'shot heard round the world.'
history_blurb: Lexington Avenue in Bedford-Stuyvesant is named for the Battle of Lexington, fought on April 19, 1775. The 'shot heard round the world' began the American Revolution. Many Brooklyn streets in Bed-Stuy were named for Revolutionary War battles and heroes.

fact_text: One of several Brooklyn streets honoring President Lincoln. This Cypress Hills road winds through a neighborhood built up in the 1880s.
history_blurb: Lincoln Avenue runs through Cypress Hills, one of Brooklyn's most geographically distinct neighborhoods set on a ridge above East New York. The street is named for President Abraham Lincoln and was part of the neighborhood's development in the 1880s.

fact_text: Lincoln Place got its name after an 1873 murder so scandalous that neighbors petitioned to rename the street.
history_blurb: In 1873, Charles Goodrich was found shot dead in his brownstone on what was then Degraw Street. His scorned lover Lizzie Lloyd King was the killer. Horrified residents petitioned the Brooklyn Common Council to rename the block, and by April 1873 it became Lincoln Place, for Abraham Lincoln.

fact_text: Lincoln Road in Prospect Lefferts Gardens was once called East New York Avenue, likely renamed around 1873 for President Lincoln.
history_blurb: Lincoln Road in Prospect Lefferts Gardens was formerly known as East New York Avenue, as shown on an 1873 Flatbush town map. The road runs through what was once the Lefferts family farmland and today forms the northern boundary of the historic Lefferts Manor district.

fact_text: Linden Boulevard is named for the American linden trees that line its perimeter. Originally called Van Brunt Street for a Dutch colonial family, it was renamed Vienna Avenue in 1887, then Lorraine Street in 1924 during anti German sentiment.
history_blurb: Linden Boulevard is named for the American linden trees that line its perimeter. Originally called Van Brunt Street for a Dutch colonial family, it was renamed Vienna Avenue in 1887, then Lorraine Street in 1924 during anti German sentiment. It finally became Linden Boulevard, one of Brooklyn's widest roads.

fact_text: Linden Street became Bushwick's first ever historic district in 2023, honoring its remarkably intact 1880s 1900s rowhouses.
history_blurb: Linden Street in Bushwick is named for linden trees. In 2023, a stretch of Linden Street became Bushwick's first ever landmarked historic district, 32 brick and brownstone rowhouses built between 1885 and 1901 by developer Samuel M. Meeker.

fact_text: Named for Philip Livingston, the only signer of the Declaration of Independence from Brooklyn, and a man with a complicated legacy.
history_blurb: Livingston Street honors Philip Livingston (1716 to 1778), a merchant, politician, and the only signer of the Declaration of Independence from Brooklyn. George Washington retreated from Long Island at Livingston's 40 acre Brooklyn Heights estate. Livingston also financed slave trading voyages.

fact_text: Formerly Locust Street, Logan Street was renamed in 1887 for the Civil War general who created Memorial Day.
history_blurb: Logan Street was formerly called Locust Street until 1887, when it was renamed for General John A. Logan (1826 to 1886), a Union commander in the Civil War. Logan is best remembered for establishing Memorial Day. He issued General Order No. 11 in 1868 designating May 30 as Decoration Day.

fact_text: This short Greenpoint street is named for the Lombardy region of Italy, in a neighborhood shaped by generations of Italian immigrants.
history_blurb: Lombardy Street in Greenpoint is one of several short interrupted streets plotted through the neighborhood. The name references the Lombardy region of northern Italy. The surrounding East Williamsburg area was one of NYC's notable Italian-American neighborhoods from the 1800s through the 1990s.

fact_text: Lorraine Street in Red Hook was originally named Vienna Avenue after the Austrian capital. During World War I, anti German sentiment led Brooklyn to rename several streets, Hamburg Avenue became Wilson Avenue, Dresden Street became Highland Place, and Vienna Avenue became Lorraine Street.
history_blurb: Lorraine Street in Red Hook was originally named Vienna Avenue after the Austrian capital. During World War I, anti German sentiment led Brooklyn to rename several streets, Hamburg Avenue became Wilson Avenue, Dresden Street became Highland Place, and Vienna Avenue became Lorraine Street.

fact_text: Lott Avenue in Brownsville honors the Lott family, Dutch settlers who farmed Flatlands for over 200 years.
history_blurb: Lott Avenue is named for the Lott family, prominent Dutch settlers in the Flatlands area of Brooklyn. The Hendrick I. Lott House, built in 1720 and expanded in 1800, is one of Brooklyn's oldest surviving residences and a New York City landmark.

fact_text: Lott Street also honors the Lott family, whose 1720 farmhouse in Marine Park is one of Brooklyn's oldest surviving buildings.
history_blurb: Lott Street, like Lott Avenue, is named for the Lott family of Flatlands. The Hendrick I. Lott House at 1940 East 36th Street, built around 1720, served as both a home and a working farm.

fact_text: Named for the colonial couples who strolled this Brooklyn Heights lane on summer evenings.
history_blurb: Love Lane began as a Native American trail to the East River and became a romantic walking path in Dutch colonial days, when couples strolled down to the water's edge. One legend ties the name to Sarah DeBevoise, a popular local woman courted by a parade of suitors.

fact_text: Luquer Street in Carroll Gardens honors Nicholas Luquer, a prominent 19th century Brooklyn civic leader and banker.
history_blurb: Luquer Street is named for Nicholas Luquer, a prominent figure in 19th century Brooklyn who served in civic roles and was associated with the area's business community. The street runs through Carroll Gardens.

fact_text: Lynch Street in Williamsburg was named for Thomas Lynch Jr., a South Carolina signer of the Declaration of Independence.
history_blurb: Lynch Street was named around 1846 as part of a wave of street namings honoring signers of the Declaration of Independence. Thomas Lynch Jr. (1749 to 1779) was the youngest signer from South Carolina. He was lost at sea shortly after the Revolution.

fact_text: MacDonough Street honors Commodore Thomas Macdonough, whose 1814 victory at Lake Champlain saved New York from British invasion.
history_blurb: MacDonough Street is named for Commodore Thomas Macdonough (1783 to 1825), the naval officer who defeated the British fleet at the Battle of Lake Champlain on September 11, 1814. His decisive victory helped end the War of 1812 and prevented a British invasion from Canada.

fact_text: MacDougal Street in Brooklyn honors Alexander McDougall, the Scottish born Sons of Liberty leader who was jailed for criticizing the British.
history_blurb: MacDougal Street is named for Alexander McDougall (1732 to 1786), a Scottish born patriot and leader of the Sons of Liberty. He was jailed in 1770 for publishing a broadside criticizing the New York Assembly and later served as a general in the Continental Army.

fact_text: Mackay Place is one of Manhattan Beach's alphabetically ordered streets laid out by developer Austin Corbin in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Mackay Place sits in Manhattan Beach's alphabetical grid of streets. The 'M' street may be named for John William Mackay (1831 to 1902), an Irish-American mining magnate who became one of the wealthiest men in America through Nevada silver mines.

fact_text: Macon Street in Bed-Stuy was named for Nathaniel Macon, a North Carolina congressman who served as Speaker of the House.
history_blurb: Macon Street is named for Nathaniel Macon (1757 to 1837), a Revolutionary War veteran who represented North Carolina in Congress for 37 years. He served as Speaker of the House (1801 to 1807) and president pro tempore of the Senate.

fact_text: Madison Street honors James Madison, the 'Father of the Constitution' and fourth President of the United States.
history_blurb: Madison Street is named for James Madison (1751 to 1836), the fourth President of the United States. Known as the 'Father of the Constitution,' he was the principal author of the Bill of Rights and cowrote The Federalist Papers with Alexander Hamilton and John Jay.

fact_text: Main Street in DUMBO marks the site of the original Brooklyn ferry landing, the spot where Brooklyn was born.
history_blurb: Main Street in DUMBO marks the site of the original Fulton Ferry landing, where Robert Fulton's steam ferry began regular service between Brooklyn and Manhattan in 1814. The street was the commercial heart of the village of Brooklyn before the borough grew.

fact_text: Named for Ralph Malbone, a grocer who farmed the surrounding land in the early 1800s.
history_blurb: Malbone Street honors Ralph Malbone, a merchant's descendant who came to Brooklyn in 1809 and farmed nearby land. Most of the street was renamed Empire Boulevard after the deadly 1918 Malbone Street subway wreck, but a short stretch keeps the old name.

fact_text: Malcolm X Boulevard was renamed from Reid Avenue in 1985 to honor the civil rights leader who was a powerful voice in Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Malcolm X Boulevard was formerly Reid Avenue. It was renamed in 1985 for Malcolm X (1925 to 1965), the civil rights leader and Nation of Islam minister who was a powerful orator and advocate for Black empowerment.

fact_text: Marcus Garvey Boulevard was renamed from Sumner Avenue in 1987 to honor the Jamaican born Black nationalist leader.
history_blurb: Marcus Garvey Boulevard was formerly Sumner Avenue. It was renamed in 1987 for Marcus Garvey (1887 to 1940), the Jamaican born leader of the Universal Negro Improvement Association who championed Black pride and Pan-Africanism. His movement had a massive following in Brooklyn during the 1920s.

fact_text: Maple Street in Prospect-Lefferts Gardens is one of several tree named streets radiating from Prospect Park.
history_blurb: Maple Street is named for the maple tree. It runs through Prospect-Lefferts Gardens, a neighborhood known for its elegant housing stock and proximity to Prospect Park. The tree themed naming pattern extends to nearby Linden, Hawthorne, and Fenimore streets.

fact_text: Marconi Place honors Guglielmo Marconi, the Italian inventor who won the Nobel Prize for pioneering wireless telegraphy.
history_blurb: Marconi Place is named for Guglielmo Marconi (1874 to 1937), the Italian electrical engineer who won the 1909 Nobel Prize in Physics for his development of long distance radio transmission. The street honors his contributions in a neighborhood with deep Italian-American roots.

fact_text: Marine Avenue in Bay Ridge takes its name from its proximity to the waterfront along the Narrows, the strait between Brooklyn and Staten Island.
history_blurb: Marine Avenue in Bay Ridge takes its name from its proximity to the waterfront along the Narrows, the strait between Brooklyn and Staten Island. Bay Ridge has a long maritime history dating to its days as the village of Yellow Hook.

fact_text: Marion Street was named for Francis Marion, the 'Swamp Fox' of the American Revolution who pioneered guerrilla warfare.
history_blurb: Marion Street is named for Brigadier General Francis Marion (c. 1732 to 1795), the South Carolina militia leader known as the 'Swamp Fox.' His guerrilla tactics against the British during the Revolution made him a folk hero and influenced American military strategy for generations.

fact_text: Market Street in Williamsburg was named for the busy markets that once served the neighborhood's commercial waterfront.
history_blurb: Market Street in South Williamsburg references the markets that operated near the East River waterfront in the 19th century. The area was a major commercial hub before the Williamsburg Bridge opened in 1903 and transformed the neighborhood.

fact_text: Marlborough Road in Flatbush was named for the Duke of Marlborough, part of the English themed street names in Victorian era Flatbush.
history_blurb: Marlborough Road is named for the Duke of Marlborough, the famous English military title. The street runs through Flatbush's historic district, where Victorian era developers gave streets English names to appeal to upwardly mobile homebuyers in the 1890s.

fact_text: Marshall Street in Bushwick likely honors John Marshall, the longest serving Chief Justice who established judicial review.
history_blurb: Marshall Street is likely named for John Marshall (1755 to 1835), the fourth Chief Justice of the United States who served for 34 years. His landmark ruling in Marbury v. Madison (1803) established the principle of judicial review.

fact_text: Named for one of Flatbush's original Dutch settler families. The Martenses have been Brooklyn landowners since the 1600s.
history_blurb: Martense Street honors the Martense family, early Dutch colonial settlers of Flatbush. The family owned land in Kings County from the 1700s onward. A section of Borough Park near Green-Wood Cemetery was once called Martense.

fact_text: This tiny Bed-Stuy block became the first street in all of NYC named for Dr. King back in 1974.
history_blurb: In 1974, this short block between Marcy and Tompkins Avenues in Bedford-Stuyvesant was renamed from Floyd Street to honor Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., making it the first street in New York City to bear his name.

fact_text: Named for the Mespeatches, a Lenape sub tribe, the word likely means 'at the bad water place' for the area's swampy terrain.
history_blurb: Maspeth Avenue takes its name from the Mespeatches, a sub tribe of the Lenape people who inhabited the area before European settlement. The name may translate to 'at the bad water place,' referring to the stagnant swamps. Dutch settlers established a colony here in 1642.

fact_text: Originally called Remsen Street, Maujer Street was renamed in 1937 for Daniel Maujer, a local alderman and landowner.
history_blurb: Maujer Street was originally called Remsen Street, after farmer Abraham Remsen. On April 30, 1937, it was renamed for Daniel Maujer, an alderman in the old 15th Ward who owned land at the junction of Remsen and Union Avenue.

fact_text: Once called Gravesend Avenue, McDonald Avenue was controversially renamed in 1933 for a beloved Brooklyn court clerk.
history_blurb: McDonald Avenue was originally Gravesend Avenue, a historic route connecting Brooklyn to the colonial town of Gravesend. It was renamed in 1933 for John R. McDonald (1871 to 1932), chief clerk at the Brooklyn Surrogate's Court. The Gravesend Chamber of Commerce opposed the change, arguing it erased a link to Lady Deborah Moody's 1645 colony.

fact_text: Meadow Street in East Williamsburg is a descriptive street name likely referencing the open meadows and grasslands that existed in the area before urbanization.
history_blurb: Meadow Street in East Williamsburg is a descriptive street name likely referencing the open meadows and grasslands that existed in the area before urbanization. The street's buildings date primarily to the 1920s.

fact_text: Once called Ralph Street, Menahan Street was renamed for Patrick J. Menahan, a corset manufacturer who had his shop right on the block.
history_blurb: Menahan Street was formerly known as Ralph Street, likely named for early landowner Ralph Patchen. It was renamed for Patrick J. Menahan, whose corset manufacturing business operated on the street in the early 1880s.

fact_text: Folk legend Woody Guthrie lived at 3520 Mermaid Avenue from 1943, inspiring the Billy Bragg and Wilco album named after the street.
history_blurb: Mermaid Avenue is one of three ocean themed thoroughfares in Coney Island, alongside Neptune Avenue and Surf Avenue. Folk legend Woody Guthrie lived at 3520 Mermaid Avenue from 1943, inspiring the Billy Bragg and Wilco album named after the street.

fact_text: Named for one of Greenpoint's founding families, Jean Miserol, a French Huguenot, arrived in New Amsterdam in 1663.
history_blurb: Meserole Street honors the Meserole family, one of Greenpoint's founding families. Jean Miserol, a French Huguenot, came to New Amsterdam on April 16, 1663. The family was among the original five families who settled in Bushwick.

fact_text: Middagh Street is named after a pre Revolutionary War family. According to local legend, a Miss Middagh replaced neighboring street signs with fruit and plant names (Pineapple, Orange, Cranberry) out of spite for aristocratic neighbors, and those names stuck.
history_blurb: Middagh Street is named after a pre Revolutionary War family. According to local legend, a Miss Middagh replaced neighboring street signs with fruit and plant names (Pineapple, Orange, Cranberry) out of spite for aristocratic neighbors, and those names stuck.

fact_text: Named for Arthur Middleton of South Carolina, one of the 56 signers of the Declaration of Independence.
history_blurb: Middleton Street in Williamsburg is named for Arthur Middleton (1742 to 1787), a signer of the Declaration of Independence from South Carolina. Many cross streets in South Williamsburg honor the signers as part of a patriotic naming tradition.

fact_text: Midwood Street's name comes from the Dutch 'Midwout' meaning 'middle woods'. It sat in the forest halfway between Bushwick and Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Midwood Street takes its name from the Middle Dutch word 'Midwout,' meaning 'middle woods.' Dutch settlers in the 1650s used this name for the dense woodland located midway between Boswyck (Bushwick) and Breukelen (Brooklyn). The area remained largely rural until the 1890s.

fact_text: A quiet East New York street. Its name may reference the English town of Milford but the exact origin is undocumented.
history_blurb: Milford Street is located in East New York. The street's naming origin is not well documented. It may reference Milford, a common English place name meaning 'mill ford,' fitting the area's agricultural past.

fact_text: Named for the colonial era tidal mills that ground grain using the power of Jamaica Bay's rising and falling tides.
history_blurb: Mill Avenue references the tidal mills that operated in the Mill Basin area during the colonial era. The Gerritsen family built a gristmill in the 1640s that harnessed Jamaica Bay's tides to grind grain. The mill ran continuously until 1934.

fact_text: Mill Street's name reflects Brooklyn's agricultural past, when water powered or tidal mills dotted the waterfront landscape.
history_blurb: Mill Street, like other mill named roads in Brooklyn, marks the location of former colonial era gristmills. The name reflects Brooklyn's agricultural past, when water powered or tidal mills dotted the waterfront landscape.

fact_text: Named for Horace A. Miller, a 19th century builder who helped develop East New York after its original developer went bust.
history_blurb: Miller Avenue in East New York is named for Horace A. Miller, a 19th century resident and builder. After developer John R. Pitkin's original plans for the neighborhood failed, Miller and James Butler repurchased the land and developed the area.

fact_text: Named for 1840s ship chandler Daniel Milton. This Greenpoint street is famous for its gorgeous row of historic houses.
history_blurb: Milton Street in Greenpoint is named for Daniel Milton, an 1840s ship chandler. Developer Thomas C. Smith later purchased land from Governor Samuel Tilden and built most of the row houses on the south side over 15 years, creating one of Brooklyn's finest streetscapes.

fact_text: Moffat Street in Bushwick was likely named for a local landowner. The Moffat family held property in the area.
history_blurb: Moffat Street in Bushwick is likely named for the Moffat family, local property owners during the neighborhood's development. Bushwick transitioned from Dutch farmland to a dense residential area in the mid to late 1800s.

fact_text: Monroe Place in Brooklyn Heights honors James Monroe, the fifth president whose 'Monroe Doctrine' warned Europe away from the Americas.
history_blurb: Monroe Place in Brooklyn Heights is named for James Monroe (1758 to 1831), the fifth President of the United States. His Monroe Doctrine of 1823 declared the Western Hemisphere closed to further European colonization and became a cornerstone of American foreign policy.

fact_text: Monroe Street in Bed-Stuy also honors President James Monroe, part of Brooklyn's tradition of naming streets for Founding Fathers.
history_blurb: Monroe Street in Bedford-Stuyvesant is named for President James Monroe. The street runs through a neighborhood where many streets honor presidents and other political figures from the early American republic.

fact_text: Montgomery Place in Park Slope is one of Brooklyn's most beautiful blocks, named for the general who died storming Quebec in 1775.
history_blurb: Montgomery Place is named for Brigadier General Richard Montgomery (1738 to 1775), an Irish born Continental Army officer killed leading the assault on Quebec City on December 31, 1775. The street in Park Slope is celebrated as one of the most architecturally distinguished blocks in NYC.

fact_text: Montgomery Street also honors General Richard Montgomery, the first American general killed in the Revolution.
history_blurb: Montgomery Street is named for General Richard Montgomery (1738 to 1775), the first American general killed in the Revolutionary War. His death at the Battle of Quebec made him a martyr for the patriot cause. Congress ordered a monument in his honor at St. Paul's Chapel in Manhattan.

fact_text: Moore Street in Bushwick was named for a local landowner. The Moore family held property in the area during its colonial era.
history_blurb: Moore Street in Bushwick is named for the Moore family, early property owners in the area. The street runs through a neighborhood that was originally part of the Dutch settlement of Boswijck, established in 1661.

fact_text: Mother Gaston Boulevard was renamed from Stone Avenue in 1988 to honor Rosetta 'Mother' Gaston, a tireless Brownsville community activist.
history_blurb: Mother Gaston Boulevard was formerly Stone Avenue. It was renamed in 1988 for Rosetta 'Mother' Gaston (1891 to 1981), a Brownsville community activist who spent decades fighting for better housing, education, and social services in one of Brooklyn's most underserved neighborhoods.

fact_text: Moultrie Street honors General William Moultrie, the Revolutionary War hero whose palmetto log fort gave South Carolina its nickname.
history_blurb: Moultrie Street is named for General William Moultrie (1730 to 1805), who defended Sullivan's Island during the 1776 British attack on Charleston. His fort, made of palmetto logs, absorbed British cannonballs, giving South Carolina its 'Palmetto State' nickname.

fact_text: Narrows Avenue takes its name from the Narrows, the tidal strait between Brooklyn and Staten Island that guards New York Harbor.
history_blurb: Narrows Avenue in Bay Ridge is named for the Narrows, the mile wide strait separating Brooklyn from Staten Island. The Narrows has been a strategic chokepoint since colonial times, guarded by Fort Hamilton. The Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge spans it today.

fact_text: Nassau Street is named for the House of Nassau-Orange, the Dutch royal family. William of Orange was both Dutch Stadtholder and King of England when England took over New Netherland.
history_blurb: Nassau Street is named for the House of Nassau-Orange, the Dutch royal family. William of Orange was both Dutch Stadtholder and King of England when England took over New Netherland. The name Nassau appears throughout New York as a legacy of Dutch colonial rule.

fact_text: Navy Street takes its name from the Brooklyn Navy Yard, which it borders. The yard was established in 1801 and built some of the Navy's most famous ships.
history_blurb: Navy Street takes its name from the Brooklyn Navy Yard, which it borders. The yard was established in 1801 and built some of the Navy's most famous ships. At its WWII peak, 70,000 workers, many of them women, built warships here.

fact_text: Neilson Street in Carroll Gardens may honor John Neilson, a New Jersey patriot who fought at the Battle of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Neilson Street is likely named for John Neilson (1745 to 1833), a New Jersey patriot and militia officer who fought in the Battle of Brooklyn in 1776. He later served in the Continental Congress.

fact_text: Nelson Street in Carroll Gardens may honor Admiral Horatio Nelson, the British naval hero of Trafalgar, or a local family.
history_blurb: Nelson Street's namesake is uncertain. It may honor Admiral Horatio Nelson (1758 to 1805), Britain's greatest naval hero who died at the Battle of Trafalgar, or a local family. British military names appear throughout Brooklyn's older neighborhoods.

fact_text: New Jersey Avenue in East New York honors the Garden State, one of several state named streets in the area.
history_blurb: New Jersey Avenue is named for the state of New Jersey. It sits in East New York alongside other state named streets like Pennsylvania and Louisiana Avenues. This naming pattern was common in 19th century developments.

fact_text: New York Avenue in Crown Heights honors the state itself, and runs through one of Brooklyn's most culturally vibrant neighborhoods.
history_blurb: New York Avenue is named for the state of New York. The avenue runs through Crown Heights, home to the annual West Indian Day Parade, which draws over a million participants.

fact_text: Newkirk Avenue honors the Newkirk family, Dutch settlers whose farm once occupied the Flatbush area.
history_blurb: Newkirk Avenue is named for the Newkirk family, Dutch colonial settlers in the Flatbush area of Brooklyn. The family were prominent landowners for generations. The Newkirk name also graces a subway station on the B/Q line.

fact_text: Newport Street in Brownsville likely takes its name from Newport, either the Rhode Island city or an English town.
history_blurb: Newport Street in Brownsville is likely named for the city of Newport, Rhode Island, or the town of Newport in England. English and American place names were commonly used for Brooklyn streets during 19th century development.

fact_text: Nichols Avenue in East New York was likely named for a local family. The Nichols held property in the area.
history_blurb: Nichols Avenue in East New York is likely named for a local landowning family. The street runs through a neighborhood that was rapidly developed in the late 19th century when elevated rail lines made the area accessible.

fact_text: Named after Ann White, wife of merchant Captain Thomas White, because other merchants' wives already had streets named for them.
history_blurb: Ann Street is one of the oldest streets in NYC, appearing on maps as early as 1728. It runs three blocks in the Financial District from Broadway to Gold Street. P.T. Barnum's famous American Museum stood at the corner of Ann Street and Broadway in the 19th century.

fact_text: Named for John Jacob Astor, once the richest person in America, who built his fortune starting with beaver fur.
history_blurb: John Jacob Astor arrived from Germany with $25 and seven flutes, then built a fortune in the beaver fur trade. He developed this area as an upper class neighborhood in the 1820s and donated the Astor Library to the city. The subway station still features decorative beaver tiles honoring the source of his wealth.

fact_text: Named for Reverend Henry Barclay, who became the second rector of Trinity Church in 1746.
history_blurb: Barclay Street honors the second rector of Trinity Church, reflecting the church's enormous influence on Lower Manhattan street names. The street later became home to the Barclay-Vesey Building, one of the first Art Deco skyscrapers, designed in 1923 by Ralph Walker for the New York Telephone Company.

fact_text: Originally Orange Street at the heart of the Five Points slum, renamed in 1853 for Lt. Col. Charles Baxter, killed in the Mexican-American War.
history_blurb: Baxter Street was the primary street forming the notorious Five Points intersection, one of the most infamous slums in American history. The neighborhood grew around the drained Collect Pond and became densely populated with Irish, African American, and Italian immigrants.

fact_text: Not named for a beach. It's a corruption of "Bache," for Paul Bache, son in law of landowner Anthony Lispenard.
history_blurb: Beach Street was named around 1790 when Trinity Church resolved to name streets on church lands. The name is a corruption of Paul Bache, a son in law of the prominent Lispenard family who owned farmland south of present day Canal Street. The street sits in Tribeca, once part of the large land grant given to Trinity Church by Queen Anne in 1705.

fact_text: Originally Bever Graft (Beaver Canal) in Dutch New Amsterdam, named for the beaver pelts that were the colony's chief currency.
history_blurb: Originally called Bever Graft by the Dutch, this street dates to as early as 1639 in New Amsterdam. The canal was filled in by 1693 and became a street. On September 8, 1664, Dutch forces marched down Beaver Street to board ships for the Netherlands, marking the transfer of New Amsterdam to English control.

fact_text: Named for Joanna Graham Bethune, a philanthropist who cofounded the New York Orphan Asylum with Mrs. Alexander Hamilton.
history_blurb: Bethune Street is one of the few Greenwich Village streets named for a woman. Joanna Graham Bethune (1770 to 1860) was a pioneering educator who opened the first school for young ladies and cofounded the New York Orphan Asylum with Mrs. Alexander Hamilton in 1806. The street was previously called Southampton Road.

fact_text: Originally Brugh Straat in Dutch, named for bridges spanning the Heere Gracht canal along present day Broad Street.
namesake: Bridge over the Heere Gracht canal
history_blurb: Bridge Street dates to 1658 in New Amsterdam, named for one of three bridges crossing the Heere Gracht (Gentlemen's Canal). After the English conquest of 1664, the canal was filled and the bridges removed, but the street kept its name as part of the landmark Street Plan of New Amsterdam.

fact_text: Named for the Heere Gracht, a Dutch canal that ran down its center before being filled in 1676.
history_blurb: Broad Street was originally the Heere Gracht (canal of the lords), an inlet from the East River that the Dutch lined with houses. After vendors clogged it with boats, the canal was filled in 1676, leaving an unusually broad street that kept the name.

fact_text: While many nearby streets were named by Trinity Church for rectors and wardens, the specific origin of the Carlisle name is not well documented in available historical sources.
history_blurb: Carlisle Street is a small street in the Financial District near the World Trade Center site. While many nearby streets were named by Trinity Church for rectors and wardens, the specific origin of the Carlisle name is not well documented in available historical sources.

fact_text: Named after Charles Christopher Amos, who inherited the old Admiral Peter Warren estate the street passed through.
history_blurb: Charles Street takes its name from Charles Christopher Amos, who acquired the old Admiral Peter Warren estate through family inheritance. Amos also gave his names to nearby Christopher Street and the former Amos Street (now West 10th Street). The street is lined with intact 19th century rowhouses in the Greenwich Village Historic District.

fact_text: Named for Charles Christopher Amos, the same landowner who gave his first name to nearby Charles Street.
history_blurb: Christopher Street is the oldest street in the West Village, running along the southern boundary of Admiral Sir Peter Warren's colonial estate. It received its current name in 1799 when the estate passed to Charles Christopher Amos. It is most famous as the site of the Stonewall Inn, where the 1969 riots launched the modern gay rights movement.

fact_text: Named for Trinity Church, the historic Anglican parish founded in 1697 that once owned vast tracts of Lower Manhattan.
history_blurb: Church Street takes its name from Trinity Church, one of Manhattan's oldest and most influential institutions. Founded in 1697 under a charter from King William III, Trinity Church once owned vast tracts of Lower Manhattan. The current Gothic Revival structure, designed by Richard Upjohn, was completed in 1846.

fact_text: Named for General Matthew Clarkson, a Revolutionary War soldier who served as aide to Benedict Arnold before Arnold's defection.
history_blurb: Matthew Clarkson (1758 to 1825) served in the Revolution, was president of the Bank of New York, and introduced a bill for the gradual abolition of slavery in New York. The West Village street still retains its original Belgian block pavement between West and Washington Streets.

fact_text: Formerly Marion Street, renamed in 1908 to honor President Grover Cleveland after his death that year.
history_blurb: Cleveland Place was renamed from Marion Street following the death of President Grover Cleveland in 1908. The renaming coincided with major street changes when the Williamsburg Bridge caused congestion and Delancey Street was extended west, reshaping the Nolita/Little Italy street grid.

fact_text: Named for Thomas Collister, who served as sexton of Trinity Church from 1790 to 1816.
history_blurb: Collister Street was laid out in 1808 and named for Trinity Church's sexton, reflecting the church's pervasive influence on Tribeca street names. It runs just two blocks from Beach to Laight Streets. The street has no addresses that don't also front on one of its three cross streets.

fact_text: Manhattan's longest true alley, laid out in 1817 and named for the prominent Dutch colonial Van Cortlandt family.
history_blurb: Cortlandt Alley was laid out in 1817 through property owned by John Jay and others, providing service access between White and Canal Streets. Named for the Van Cortlandt family who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1637, it is lined with 1860s 1890s brick warehouses and is famously the most filmed alley in New York City.

fact_text: Named for Oloff Van Cortlandt, a Dutch colonial brewer and burgomaster of New Amsterdam from 1655 to 1666.
history_blurb: Cortlandt Street was laid out around 1735 and named for Oloff Stevense Van Cortlandt, who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1637 as a soldier for the Dutch West India Company. He became a wealthy brewer and served as burgomaster. His son Stephanus became New York's first native born mayor.

fact_text: Named for William Bedlow Crosby, a 19th century millionaire who inherited hundreds of SoHo acres from Henry Rutgers.
history_blurb: Crosby Street runs six blocks through SoHo from Bleecker to Howard Street. William Bedlow Crosby was the grand nephew and chief inheritor of Henry Rutgers, who owned vast acreage in the area. In the mid 1800s the street was a commercial hub; by the 1970s it became the heart of SoHo's artist loft movement.

fact_text: Named for Elias Desbrosses, a Huguenot merchant and Trinity Church warden whose pier saw both Washington and Lincoln's body arrive.
history_blurb: Elias Desbrosses (1718 to 1778), son of a 17th century Huguenot immigrant, began as a confectioner before becoming a West Indies trader. The Desbrosses Street pier saw two historic arrivals: George Washington landed there en route to command the Continental Army, and Abraham Lincoln's body arrived by ferry for its 1865 funeral procession.

fact_text: Named for Dirck Dey, a Dutch soldier who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1641 and owned land from Broadway to the Hudson.
history_blurb: Dey Street is named for Dirck Dey, who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1641 as a soldier for the Dutch West India Company. His family owned about five and a half acres from Broadway to the Hudson. The street was laid out and paved in 1749; its western end was later demolished for the World Trade Center.

fact_text: Named for the 1765 boundary it marked between the De Lancey estate and the Rutgers farm.
namesake: the De Lancey / Rutgers farm boundary
history_blurb: Division Street traces the line agreed in 1765 dividing James De Lancey's estate to the north from Henry Rutgers' farm to the south. The strip itself was once a no man's land used as a rope walk for twisting hemp into rope.

fact_text: Named for Georges Dominick, a French Huguenot refugee who became a vestryman at Trinity Church in the 1780s.
history_blurb: Georges Dominick was born in La Rochelle, France in 1739 and arrived in New York in 1742. He became a merchant, served as a Captain in the Second New York Militia in 1775, and was a Trinity Church vestryman from 1787 to 1792. The street was laid out in the early 1820s through what had been Lispenard's Meadow.

fact_text: Named for James Duane, a Revolutionary War leader and the first mayor of New York City after independence.
history_blurb: James Duane was a delegate to the Continental Congress, a judge of the U.S. District Court, and New York's first post independence mayor. Duane Park, the second oldest public park in NYC, was purchased from Trinity Church in 1797 at the street's oddly wide intersection.

fact_text: A short Financial District lane honoring the Dutch settlers who founded New Amsterdam, Colgate started here at No. 6 in 1806.
history_blurb: Dutch Street is a small lane in the Financial District that honors New York's Dutch colonial heritage. It first appeared on maps around 1776. In 1806, William Colgate opened his first soap shop at 6 Dutch Street, founding what became the Colgate-Palmolive Company.

fact_text: Named in 1817 for Lt. Joseph C. Eldridge, killed in a War of 1812 ambush, later home to a landmark 1887 synagogue.
history_blurb: Originally called Third Street on the DeLancey Farm Grid, the street was renamed in 1817 for Lt. Joseph C. Eldridge, whose unit was ambushed by British allied forces during the War of 1812. The street became the heart of Jewish immigrant life on the Lower East Side, home to the landmark 1887 Eldridge Street Synagogue.

fact_text: Likely named for Elizabeth Rynders, granddaughter of colonial figure Jacob Leisler, later the center of Sicilian immigrant life.
history_blurb: The southern part of Elizabeth Street was built in 1755 and extended north in 1816. The street was likely named for Elizabeth Rynders, connected to the Leisler-Bayard families. It became the center of Sicilian immigrant life in Little Italy before transitioning to Chinatown.

fact_text: A renamed section of Beach Street honoring John Ericsson, Swedish inventor of the Civil War ironclad USS Monitor, who lived here.
history_blurb: Ericsson Place is a one block section of Beach Street between Varick and Hudson, renamed in 1922 to honor John Ericsson, inventor of the screw propeller and designer of the USS Monitor. Ericsson lived at 36 Beach Street from 1867 until his death in 1889.

fact_text: Renamed in 1827 from Garden Street after the Merchants Exchange Building was built at nearby Wall and William Streets.
history_blurb: Exchange Place dates to 1657 in the Dutch colony, incorporating parts of two colonial roads: Heere Dwars Straet and Tuyn Straet (Garden Street).
