The surrounding area was once the Schaap Weyte (sheep pasture). It was renamed Exchange Place in 1827 when the Merchants Exchange was built nearby.

fact_text: Named for Benjamin Fletcher, the controversial colonial governor of New York from 1692 to 1697.
history_blurb: Fletcher Street in the Financial District honors Benjamin Fletcher, colonial governor of New York (1692 to 1697). Fletcher passed the Ministry Act of 1693 establishing Anglicanism in New York and built the first Trinity Church. His governorship was also notorious for encouraging piracy as an economic development tool.

fact_text: Renamed in 1817 from East 2nd Street to honor Lt. Col. Benjamin Forsyth, killed in the War of 1812.
history_blurb: Originally East 2nd Street, the road was renamed in 1817 for Lt. Col. Benjamin Forsyth of North Carolina, who died from wounds sustained fighting in Canada during the War of 1812. North Carolina also named Forsyth County after him. The street later filled with tenements and synagogues as immigrants flooded the Lower East Side.

fact_text: Renamed in 1837 from Great Kill Road to honor Revolutionary War hero General Peter Gansevoort, grandfather of Herman Melville.
history_blurb: Originally a Lenape trading path called Sapohanikan, the road became Great Kill Road before being renamed in 1837 for General Peter Gansevoort, a Revolutionary War hero. His grandson Herman Melville later worked as a customs inspector on docks at the foot of Gansevoort Street.

fact_text: A tiny Greenwich Village lane likely named for a local family. The name long predates any LGBTQ association.
history_blurb: Gay Street first appeared in city records on April 23, 1827, when Greenwich Village was being settled by merchants fleeing yellow fever. Most historians believe it was named for a local family, as many nearby streets bear family names. The name long predates any association with LGBTQ culture in the Village.

fact_text: Named for Abraham Gouverneur, a Huguenot merchant whose descendants include Gouverneur Morris, framer of the Constitution.
history_blurb: Gouverneur Lane honors Abraham Gouverneur, a French Huguenot refugee who became a prosperous merchant and married into the Leisler family. Despite the spelling, the name has no connection to any New York governor. His descendants include Gouverneur Morris, a framer of the U.S. Constitution.

fact_text: Also named for Huguenot merchant Abraham Gouverneur, often confused with a governor, but named for a merchant family.
history_blurb: Like Gouverneur Lane, this street honors Abraham Gouverneur, the Huguenot merchant and political activist. The Gouverneur name extends to the street, lane, and slips in Lower Manhattan. The family's prominence in colonial New York left a lasting mark on the city's geography.

fact_text: Named for lawyer Samuel Jones, "Father of the New York Bar," who insisted any street through his land bear his name.
history_blurb: Samuel Jones donated the land for this street on the condition it be named after him. Since a Jones Street already existed in Greenwich Village, his street was called "Great Jones" due to its greater width. In the 1840s it was one of Manhattan's most prestigious addresses.

fact_text: Originally Elm Street, renamed for Nathanael Greene, one of Washington's most trusted Revolutionary War generals.
history_blurb: Originally called Elm Street, it was renamed for Major General Nathanael Greene, one of Washington's most trusted commanders in the Revolutionary War. By 1850 the street was a prosperous residential block; by 1870 it had become a major red light district. Entrepreneurs later built the famous cast iron warehouses that define SoHo today.

fact_text: Named for Greenwich Village, from the Dutch "Groenwijck" meaning "Green District". It was the road to the village.
history_blurb: Greenwich Street was laid out in 1761 as the main route from Lower Manhattan to the then independent village of Greenwich. Its name comes from the Dutch "Groenwijck" meaning "Green District." When yellow fever struck in the 1790s, New Yorkers fled north along this road to Greenwich Village.

fact_text: Renamed multiple times, from Columbia to Cozine to Burrows, it finally became Grove Street for its nearby orchards.
history_blurb: Originally Columbia Street in 1799 when it crossed apple and cherry orchards, the street became Cozine Street, then Burrows Street (for War of 1812 naval hero William Burrows). To avoid confusion with nearby Barrow Street, it was renamed Grove Street in the 1820s.

fact_text: Named in 1714 for Britain's House of Hanover when King George I took the throne. The Great Fire of 1835 broke out here.
history_blurb: Hanover Square was named around 1714 to honor the House of Hanover when George I became King of England. It was the center of New York's 18th century printing and retail trade. The Great Fire of 1835, which destroyed much of Lower Manhattan, broke out here on December 16 of that year.

fact_text: Named for the House of Hanover, the German royal dynasty that took the British throne with King George I in 1714.
history_blurb: Hanover Square and its surrounding streets were named in tribute to the House of Hanover when George I ascended to the British throne in 1714. For much of the 18th century, Hanover Square was the center of New York's printing trade and retail business.

fact_text: Named after the Harrison Brewery, which occupied the land starting in 1765 in what is now Tribeca.
history_blurb: The Harrison Brewery occupied this Tribeca land beginning in 1765 and lent its name to the street. The land was originally part of the farm of Annetje Jans, granted by Dutch Director General Van Twiller in 1636. Today the street is famous for its Federal style townhouses built between 1804 and 1828, the largest collection of Federal architecture remaining in NYC.

fact_text: Named for Hester Leisler, daughter of Jacob Leisler, the colonial governor hanged for treason in 1691.
history_blurb: Hester Street is named for Hester Leisler, daughter of Jacob Leisler, the lieutenant governor of colonial New York who was controversially convicted of treason and hanged in 1691. In the late 1800s, the street became the heart of Jewish immigrant life on the Lower East Side, with thousands of pushcart vendors lining its blocks.

fact_text: Named for Revolutionary War General Horatio Gates, who lived on the block when it was named.
history_blurb: Horatio Street is named after Revolutionary War hero General Horatio Gates (1727 to 1806), who lived on the block when it was named. Gates led American troops to a decisive victory at the Battle of Saratoga but later controversially tried to oust George Washington as commander. He freed his slaves and moved to New York in 1790.

fact_text: Origin debated, possibly named for fire chief Harry Howard, though the name appeared on maps before his birth.
history_blurb: The origin of Howard Street's name is disputed. Some attribute it to Harry Howard (1822 to 1889), chief of NYC's volunteer fire department, but the name appeared on maps by the 1820s, making that impossible. In the 1800s, the street was a hub for fabric, fashion, and cast iron architecture in what is now SoHo.

fact_text: Named for Hubert Van Wagenen, a businessman connected to Trinity Church, which owned the surrounding Tribeca land.
history_blurb: Hubert Street was named in connection with Trinity Church's development of its vast land holdings in lower Manhattan. Around 1790, Trinity's Vestry resolved to name streets on its Church Farm property, and Hubert Street was among them. The surrounding area was part of the land Queen Anne granted to Trinity in 1705.

fact_text: Named after the Hudson River, itself named for explorer Henry Hudson who sailed it for the Dutch in 1609.
history_blurb: Hudson Street, laid out around 1796, takes its name from the Hudson River, named after English explorer Henry Hudson. In 1609, Hudson sailed up the river on behalf of the Dutch East India Company, mapping Manhattan Island. The street runs from Tribeca through Greenwich Village and historically served as a hub for warehouses and printing presses.

fact_text: Named after St. James the Greater, the apostle. The nearby St. James Church has served the area since 1836.
history_blurb: James Street and nearby St. James Place are named for St. James the Greater, one of the twelve apostles. The St. James Roman Catholic Church, dedicated in 1836, served the neighborhood's largely Irish immigrant community. Much of James Street was demolished between the 1930s and 1960s for public housing.

fact_text: Likely named for the Jaynes family who owned the land, not for a woman named Jane as many assume.
history_blurb: Jane Street was laid out before 1817 on land owned by the Jaynes family, and the street name probably derives from that surname rather than a woman's first name. The street has a dramatic historical connection: after Alexander Hamilton was mortally wounded in his duel with Aaron Burr, he was reportedly taken to a house on Jane Street.

fact_text: A tiny SoHo alley once called Columbian Alley, renamed around 1829, possibly for the English Channel island of Jersey.
history_blurb: Jersey Street runs east west from Mulberry to Crosby in SoHo and was laid out in the 1820s. It was briefly called Columbian Alley before being renamed Jersey Street around 1829. It is one of Manhattan's rare east west back alleys.

fact_text: Named for Dr. Gardiner Jones, who laid out this Greenwich Village lane in 1794, his in law got "Great Jones Street."
history_blurb: In 1794, Dr. Gardiner Jones laid out a short lane in Greenwich Village that took his name. His brother in law, lawyer Samuel Jones, later demanded a street named for him too. Since Jones Street already existed, the larger street was called "Great Jones Street." The two Jones Streets have confused New Yorkers ever since.

fact_text: Named in 1911 by politician "Big Tim" Sullivan for his mother's hometown of Kenmare in County Kerry, Ireland.
history_blurb: Kenmare Street did not exist before 1911, when traffic from the new Williamsburg Bridge required Delancey Street to be widened and a new street opened west of the Bowery. Tim "Big Tim" Sullivan named it after the town in County Kerry from which his mother emigrated during the Great Famine of the 1840s.

fact_text: Named for Rufus King, Founding Father and U.S. Senator from New York, not for any British monarch.
history_blurb: King Street in SoHo honors Rufus King (1755 to 1827), a Founding Father who served as delegate to the Continental Congress, U.S. Senator, and Minister to Great Britain. King was notably one of the strongest opponents of slavery among the Founders. Confusingly, an earlier King Street (now Pine Street) had honored the British monarch before being renamed after the Revolution.

fact_text: Named for William Laight, a Trinity Church vestryman and ardent anti Royalist during the American Revolution.
history_blurb: Trinity Church named this Tribeca street for William Laight, a vestryman and passionate anti Royalist during the Revolution. When Trinity developed the land around 1803, the boggy, mosquito filled area was transformed into Manhattan's most exclusive neighborhood, centered on St. John's Park.

fact_text: Named for shipping merchant Jacob Le Roy, though a romantic legend ties it to the lost son of Louis XVI hiding in New York.
history_blurb: Leroy Street, first named in 1807, honors Jacob Le Roy, a shipping merchant who ran a blockade against the British in the War of 1812. A colorful legend claims the street was named for the "Lost Dauphin" of France living incognito under the name Leroy (French for "the King"), but the timeline does not support it.

fact_text: Named for Morgan Lewis, Revolutionary War veteran and Governor of New York from 1804 to 1807.
history_blurb: Lewis Street on the Lower East Side was named for Morgan Lewis (1754 to 1844), who served in the Revolutionary War and War of 1812 and was New York's Governor from 1804 to 1807. Most of the street was eliminated between 1945 and 1959 for public housing construction; today only a single block remains near the Williamsburg Bridge.

fact_text: Renamed from Crown Street in 1793 to celebrate American liberty after the Revolution.
history_blurb: Before the Revolution this was Crown Street, named for the British monarchy. In 1793 the newly independent city renamed it Liberty Street, part of a wave of patriotic renamings that swept royal names off the map.

fact_text: Named for the Lispenard family, whose farm and meadows covered a swath of lower Manhattan starting in 1746.
history_blurb: Lispenard Street honors the Lispenard family, Leonard Lispenard purchased the land in 1746, creating a vast estate stretching to the Hudson River known as Lispenard Meadows. Nearby Leonard Street was also named for the Lispenard family. The two block street was opened in 1809.

fact_text: Named for Alexander McDougall, a Scottish born Son of Liberty jailed by the British for a seditious pamphlet in 1770.
history_blurb: MacDougal Street honors Alexander McDougall (1732 to 1786), a Scottish born merchant and original Son of Liberty who was imprisoned in 1770 for writing a pamphlet denouncing British trade restrictions. He served as a general in the Revolutionary War and became the first president of the Bank of New York. The spelling of the street differs from his surname.

fact_text: Named in 1826 after James Madison, the fourth U.S. president, a decade before Madison Avenue opened nearby.
history_blurb: Madison Street is a two way thoroughfare on the Lower East Side running from the Brooklyn Bridge entrance ramp to Grand Street. It was named in 1826 for President James Madison. Madison Square was named for him in 1814 while he was serving his second term.

fact_text: Originally George Street (for King George III), it was renamed in 1813 after the nearby Catherine Market.
history_blurb: Market Street was one of eight colonial era George Streets named for the British monarch. After the Revolution, all were renamed to shed British influence. This one became Market Street in 1813, referencing the Catherine Market, a bustling produce market at the East River dock established in 1786.

fact_text: Named from the Dutch "Marcktveldt" for New Amsterdam's first cattle market, established nearby in 1641.
history_blurb: This one block alley in the Financial District traces its name to the Dutch colonial cattle market established by Governor Willem Kieft in 1641 near present day Battery Park. The street has also been known as Petticoat Lane, Exchange Street, and Field Street. In 1688, the city's first French Huguenot church was built here.

fact_text: Named for Minetta Creek, from the Lenape "manette" (devil water), filtered through Dutch as "Mintje Kill" (little stream).
history_blurb: Minetta Lane runs through Greenwich Village from Sixth Avenue to MacDougal Street, following the path of the now buried Minetta Creek. The creek's name blends Lenape and Dutch origins. In the 1820s, the city diverted the creek into a sewer and the path along it became Minetta Lane. The area was once known as "Little Africa" for its African-American community.

fact_text: Minetta Street takes its name from the buried Minetta Creek, whose winding course its curved path still traces.
history_blurb: Minetta Street curves from Sixth Avenue to Bleecker Street in Greenwich Village, following the original course of the underground Minetta Creek. After the brook was covered in the 1820s, the path became Minetta Lane turning into Minetta Street. The two Minettas intersect halfway, forming part of the Village's signature off the grid character.

fact_text: Renamed in 1831 for President James Monroe; in the 19th century it was the heart of "Cork Row" for Irish immigrants.
history_blurb: Monroe Street runs through the Lower East Side from Catherine Street to Corlears Hook. It was renamed in 1831 for the fifth president, James Monroe. In the 19th century, it was the heart of "Cork Row," home to Irish immigrants from County Cork.

fact_text: Named for General Richard Montgomery, the Irish born Continental Army hero killed at Quebec in 1775.
history_blurb: Montgomery Street on the Lower East Side honors Major General Richard Montgomery, who was killed leading the 1775 invasion of Quebec and became one of the Revolution's first celebrated martyrs. His remains rest at St. Paul's Chapel in Manhattan.

fact_text: Originally called Beaver Lane, this Financial District street likely honors the prominent colonial Morris family of New York.
history_blurb: Morris Street in the Financial District was formerly known as Beaver Lane. The Morris family, descendants of Welsh immigrant Lewis Morris who arrived in the 1600s, were among New York's most influential colonial dynasties.

fact_text: Most likely named for Jacob Morton, a Revolutionary era militia general and prominent New York civic leader.
history_blurb: Morton Street runs through the West Village from Bleecker to the Hudson River. Most historians attribute the name to Jacob Morton, a prominent New York lawyer and long serving general of the state militia. The name was reassigned to this location in 1807 during the Village's grid planning.

fact_text: Renamed in 1982 for community activist Frank Mosco; it was formerly Cross Street at the heart of the Five Points.
history_blurb: This one block Chinatown street between Mulberry and Mott is the last remnant of Cross Street, which ran through the notorious Five Points slum. Cross Street was renamed Park Street in 1854, then Mosco Street in 1982 to honor Frank Mosco, a local activist who organized youth outreach and affordable housing.

fact_text: Named for Robert Murray, the Scottish merchant whose wife Mary famously stalled British officers during the Revolution.
history_blurb: Murray Street honors Robert Murray (1721 to 1786), a Scottish born merchant who owned a large estate in Manhattan. During the Revolution in September 1776, his wife Mary Lindley Murray reportedly entertained British officers at their home, delaying their advance long enough for Washington's troops to escape.

fact_text: Named by 1696 for the Dutch House of Orange-Nassau; in the 19th century it became New York's Newspaper Row.
history_blurb: Nassau Street was originally Kip Street, after an early Dutch settler family, before being renamed for the royal House of Orange-Nassau of the Netherlands. In the 19th century, it became New York's newspaper row. The original NY Times headquarters stood at 113 Nassau Street.

fact_text: So named because it was the first street added after the British took over New Amsterdam in 1664.
history_blurb: New Street in the Financial District earned its straightforward name as the first street laid out after the British seized New Amsterdam from the Dutch in 1664. It runs between Wall Street and Beaver Street, flanking the New York Stock Exchange.

fact_text: Named in 1790 for Bishop Benjamin Moore, president of Columbia College and father of Clement Clarke Moore.
history_blurb: North Moore Street in Tribeca was named in 1790 to honor Benjamin Moore (1748 to 1816), second bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of New York and president of Columbia College. The "North" distinguishes it from the older Moore Street near Manhattan's southern tip. The street was laid out on Trinity Church's Church Farm holdings.

fact_text: Named for an old ship slip; a man made inlet where boats docked on the East River before it was filled in.
namesake: an early East River ship slip
history_blurb: Old Slip is named for one of the colonial era slips: inlets cut into the waterfront where ships slipped in to dock and unload. The slips were filled as the shoreline expanded, but their names survive on the streets that replaced them.

fact_text: Named for Oliver De Lancey, a British loyalist brigadier exiled to England after the Revolution.
history_blurb: Oliver Street honors Oliver De Lancey (1718 to 1785), youngest son of French Huguenot immigrant Etienne De Lancey and brother of James De Lancey (namesake of Delancey Street). Oliver raised a brigade of 1,500 Loyalists during the Revolution. After the war, the family was exiled but the street kept its name.

fact_text: Renamed in 1886 from Chatham Street because it faces City Hall Park; it became famous as "Newspaper Row."
history_blurb: Park Row began as a Lenape footpath, became part of the colonial Post Road to Boston, and was named Chatham Street in 1774. It was officially renamed Park Row in 1886 for its position facing City Hall Park. In the late 1800s, it earned the nickname "Newspaper Row" as most major NYC papers headquartered along it.

fact_text: Named for Aaron Patchin, a surveyor who married the landowner's daughter, E.E. Cummings and Djuna Barnes later lived here.
history_blurb: This gated dead end street off West 10th Street was once part of Sir Peter Warren's farm, sold in 1799 to Samuel Milligan. Milligan's daughter married his surveyor, Aaron Patchin, whose name stuck to the alley. The ten brick row houses were built around 1848 as housing for Basque staff of the nearby Brevoort House hotel.

fact_text: Named for Johannes (Jan) Peck, an early Dutch settler who emigrated from the Netherlands before 1650.
history_blurb: Peck Slip was a harbor inlet on the East River named for Johannes Peck and his wife Maria, Dutch emigrants who settled in New Amsterdam before 1650. A general produce market was established here by 1660. Like all Manhattan slips, it was eventually filled in, leaving only the street name.

fact_text: Named for the Pell family, Thomas Pell signed a 1654 treaty with the Siwanoy for 50,000 acres of what became the Bronx.
history_blurb: Originally called Oak Street, Pell Street was renamed for the Pell family, prominent colonial landowners. Thomas Pell signed a 1654 treaty with the Siwanoy tribe for 50,000 acres in the Bronx and Westchester. The short, narrow Chinatown street became one of the neighborhood's original thoroughfares.

fact_text: Named for Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry, hero of the 1813 Battle of Lake Erie during the War of 1812.
history_blurb: Perry Street runs through the West Village from Seventh Avenue to the Hudson River. It was named for Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry, whose victory at the Battle of Lake Erie in 1813 made him a national hero. The area was formerly Van Ness farmland and developed into a fashionable residential street.

fact_text: Named for explorer Zebulon Pike of Pike's Peak fame, who was killed fighting the British in 1813.
history_blurb: Pike Street was previously called Charlotte Street and was renamed in the 1810s to honor Zebulon Pike (1779 to 1813), the explorer who gave his name to Pike's Peak in Colorado. Pike was killed during the War of 1812 while fighting the British in Canada. The street runs through Chinatown and the Lower East Side.

fact_text: Originally King Street in the colonial era, renamed after the Revolution for a pine grove on a nearby Dutch farm.
history_blurb: Pine Street was known as King Street on maps from 1693 to 1794. After the Revolution, the city purged royal names and this one was renamed for a pine grove on the nearby farm of early settler Jan Jansen Damen. It became a hub for banking and insurance in the 19th century.

fact_text: Named in 1834 for merchant Jacob Platt, through whose property the two block Financial District street was extended.
history_blurb: Platt Street is a short, two block street in the Financial District running from William Street to Pearl Street. It was named in 1834 for Jacob Platt, a local merchant through whose property the street was extended.

fact_text: Derived from the Dutch "Princess Straet", one of the few royal street names to survive the post Revolution purge.
history_blurb: Prince Street derives from an English colonial translation of the original Dutch "Princess Straet." After the British evacuated NYC in 1783, the city purged most royal street names, but Prince Street survived because it was on the city's periphery at the time. Today it runs through SoHo and Nolita.

fact_text: Named for Joseph Reade, a colonial warden of Trinity Church, also half the namesake of the Duane Reade pharmacy chain.
history_blurb: Reade Street is named after Joseph Reade (1694 to 1771), a prominent colonial New Yorker who was a warden of Trinity Church and a member of the governor's council. The street was mapped in 1761 on Trinity Church's Church Farm holdings. It is also half the namesake of the Duane Reade pharmacy chain, whose first warehouse sat between Duane and Reade Streets.

fact_text: Named for its proximity to Trinity Church, referring to the office of the church's rector rather than a specific person.
history_blurb: Rector Street in the Financial District takes its name from its close association with Trinity Church, whose rectors have led the parish since 1697. While nearby Vesey Street honors the first rector William Vesey by name, Rector Street refers to the clerical office itself.

fact_text: Named for Henry Rutgers, a Revolutionary War captain whose estate covered much of the Lower East Side.
history_blurb: Henry Rutgers (1745 to 1830) was a Revolutionary War captain and descendant of Dutch colonists who arrived in 1636. When the 1811 street grid divided his estate, Rutgers named this boulevard after himself and nearby Henry Street after his first name. Rutgers University in New Jersey also bears his name.

fact_text: Named for Colonel Alexander Scammell, Washington's Adjutant General, mortally wounded by British dragoons at Yorktown.
history_blurb: Scammel Street honored Colonel Alexander Scammell (1747 to 1781), Washington's Adjutant General who was wounded and captured by British dragoons at Yorktown. The street was eliminated in 1940 when the Vladeck Houses public housing project was built over its route on the Lower East Side.

fact_text: Likely named for Colonel Marinus Willett, Sheriff of New York from 1784 to 1788, who later became mayor.
history_blurb: Sheriff Street appeared on maps as early as 1797 and most likely takes its name from Colonel Marinus Willett, Sheriff of New York from 1784 to 88 and later Mayor. The street was obliterated in the 1950s 60s for public housing; only a lonely street sign beside the Williamsburg Bridge remains.

fact_text: The street was later renamed William after Willem Beekman, who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1647 with Peter Stuyvesant.
history_blurb: South William Street was originally called Mill Street for the Dutch windmill that stood along it, which also housed the first Jewish congregation in North America, Shearith Israel. The street was later renamed William after Willem Beekman, who arrived in New Amsterdam in 1647 with Peter Stuyvesant.

fact_text: Named for an actual freshwater spring that ran through Lispenard's Meadow. Its underground stream still floods basements today.
history_blurb: Spring Street takes its name from a freshwater spring that flowed through Lispenard's Meadow near present day West Broadway. The stream still runs underground and occasionally floods basements. Before 1806 the road was called Brannon Street, after a local tavern keeper.

fact_text: Originally George Street for King George III, one of eight George Streets renamed after the Revolution.
history_blurb: Spruce Street was laid out around 1725 as George Street, honoring King George III. After the Revolution, New York purged royal street names; all eight of Manhattan's George Streets were renamed. This one became Spruce Street, likely for native spruce trees.

fact_text: Originally New Bowery, renamed in 1947 for the nearby St. James Roman Catholic Church, built in 1837 for Irish immigrants.
history_blurb: St. James Place was originally called New Bowery and was renamed in 1947 after the adjacent St. James Roman Catholic Church. Built of fieldstone in 1835 to 37, the church served the neighborhood's large Irish immigrant community. The oldest Jewish cemetery in the U.S., the Chatham Square Cemetery of Shearith Israel, is also located on this street.

fact_text: Named in 1835 after the fashionable St. Mark's Church in-the-Bowery to attract wealthy buyers to new townhouses.
history_blurb: In 1835, developer Thomas E. Davis named the street after the nearby St. Mark's Church in-the-Bowery to give it an exclusive cachet. The land was originally part of Peter Stuyvesant's Bouwerij farm. By the 1960s St. Marks Place had become the epicenter of hippie and counterculture life in the East Village.

fact_text: Origin debated, possibly named for "staple" goods unloaded at nearby markets, or for landowner John J. Staples circa 1800.
history_blurb: Staple Street's origin is debated: some say it refers to goods or "staples" unloaded at nearby colonial markets, while others suggest it honors John J. Staples, a nearby property owner around 1800. The tiny Tribeca alley is famous for its 1907 skybridge connecting two buildings of the New York Hospital's House of Relief.

fact_text: Originally Copsey Street after the Lenape village of Kopsee, renamed for the State House built on the site of old Fort Amsterdam.
history_blurb: State Street was originally called Copsey Street after Kopsee, a nearby Lenape village. It was renamed by the Common Council in the 1790s after the Government House (State House) built on the site of the old Dutch Fort Amsterdam. The street approximates the island's original waterline before landfill.

fact_text: Named for its cobblestones; in 1658 it became the first paved street in New Amsterdam.
history_blurb: Stone Street earned its name in 1794 for the cobblestones first laid in 1658, which made it the first paved street in New Amsterdam. It incorporates two colonial streets, including Brewers Street, named for the breweries that once lined it.

fact_text: Named in 1799 for Major General John Sullivan, who fought at Trenton, Brandywine, and Germantown in the Revolution.
history_blurb: Sullivan Street was called Locust Street until 1799, when it was renamed for Revolutionary War Major General John Sullivan (1740 to 1795). Sullivan commanded at the Battles of Long Island, Trenton, Brandywine, and Germantown. Part of the street falls within the MacDougal-Sullivan Gardens Historic District.

fact_text: Astoria Boulevard carries the name of John Jacob Astor, then the richest man in America. Village founders renamed Hallett's Cove as Astoria in 1839 hoping to flatter him into investing. He gave about 500 dollars and never set foot in the neighborhood.
history_blurb: Astoria Boulevard takes its name from the village of Astoria, which was named in 1839 for John Jacob Astor, then the wealthiest man in America. Local founders led by Stephen Halsey chose the name hoping Astor would invest in the new village at Hallett's Cove. He contributed only a token sum and never visited the place that bears his name.

fact_text: Steinway Street is named for Henry Steinway and his piano firm Steinway and Sons, which built a complete company town in northern Astoria in the 1870s with its own factory, housing, church, and trolley line.
history_blurb: Steinway Street honors the Steinway family of piano makers. German immigrant Henry Steinway founded Steinway and Sons in 1853, and his sons built a company town in northern Astoria in the 1870s, complete with a factory, worker housing, a church, a library, and a kindergarten. The factory still makes pianos on 19th Avenue today.

fact_text: Ditmars Boulevard honors Abram Ditmars, the first mayor of Long Island City, elected in 1870. His family were Dutch farmers who worked northern Queens land for generations.
history_blurb: Ditmars Boulevard is named in honor of Abram Ditmars, who was elected the first mayor of Long Island City in 1870. The Ditmars family descended from early Dutch settlers who farmed in northern Queens for generations before the street grid arrived.

fact_text: Vernon Boulevard is named for British Admiral Edward Vernon, the same namesake as Mount Vernon. George Washington's half brother Lawrence served under Vernon at sea and brought the name to America.
history_blurb: Vernon Boulevard takes its name from Admiral Edward Vernon of the British Navy, famous for capturing Portobello in 1739. Lawrence Washington, George Washington's half brother, served under Vernon and named Mount Vernon for him. A turnpike connecting Queens to Brooklyn was named Vernon Avenue in 1842 and later became Vernon Boulevard.

fact_text: Newtown Avenue took travelers from Astoria toward the colonial town of Newtown, the English settlement of 1652 that is now Elmhurst. The whole surrounding township carried the Newtown name for over two centuries.
history_blurb: Newtown Avenue is named for the colonial town of Newtown, founded by English settlers in 1652 and known today as Elmhurst. The road led from the village of Astoria toward the town center, and the surrounding township kept the Newtown name until Queens was consolidated into New York City in 1898.

fact_text: Shore Boulevard is named for its route along the shore of Hell Gate, the turbulent East River strait whose name comes from the Dutch Hellegat. It hugs the water through Astoria Park with no buildings on either side.
namesake: The East River shore at Hell Gate
history_blurb: Shore Boulevard carries a simple descriptive name for its route along the East River shore through Astoria Park. The water it follows is Hell Gate, a strait whose name derives from the Dutch Hellegat, the treacherous channel that sank hundreds of ships before the Army Corps of Engineers dynamited its rocks in 1885.

fact_text: Hoyt Avenue preserves the name of the Hoyt family, prominent early Astoria landowners whose waterfront estates stood where Astoria Park spreads today.
history_blurb: Hoyt Avenue is named for the Hoyt family, one of the prominent families such as the Potters and Woolseys who built mansions along the Astoria waterfront in the 19th century. Their former land now lies within Astoria Park beneath the Triborough Bridge approaches.

fact_text: Victory Boulevard was renamed in 1918 to celebrate the Allied victory in World War I. Before that it was the Richmond Turnpike, a private toll road built in the 1810s as a fast route between New York and Philadelphia.
namesake: The Allied victory in World War I
history_blurb: Victory Boulevard commemorates the Allied victory in World War I. The road began as the Richmond Turnpike, chartered in 1816 under Vice President Daniel D. Tompkins as part of a fast stagecoach and ferry route between New York and Philadelphia, and was renamed for the victory in 1918.

fact_text: Hylan Boulevard honors John F. Hylan, mayor of New York from 1918 to 1925. He grew up poor, worked as a railroad motorman in Brooklyn, and was fired for nearly running over his supervisor before becoming a lawyer and mayor.
history_blurb: Hylan Boulevard is named for John Francis Hylan, the 96th mayor of New York City, who served from 1918 to 1925. A former Brooklyn elevated railroad motorman who studied law at night, Hylan championed the five cent subway fare and the city's public transit system. The boulevard was named for him in 1923, while he was still in office.

fact_text: Richmond Terrace carries Staten Island's colonial name. Richmond County was named in 1683 for Charles Lennox, Duke of Richmond, an illegitimate son of King Charles II.
history_blurb: Richmond Terrace takes its name from Richmond County, established in 1683 and named for Charles Lennox, the 1st Duke of Richmond and an illegitimate son of King Charles II. The Richmond name spread across the island to Richmond Road, Richmondtown, and Port Richmond, and the borough itself was officially the Borough of Richmond until 1975.

fact_text: Richmond Road is named for the colonial county seat of Richmondtown, which in turn honors Charles Lennox, Duke of Richmond, an illegitimate son of King Charles II. The road was one of the island's earliest highways.
history_blurb: Richmond Road led to Richmondtown, the colonial seat of Richmond County at the island's center. The county was named in 1683 for Charles Lennox, the 1st Duke of Richmond, an illegitimate son of King Charles II. The road follows one of Staten Island's oldest colonial routes.

fact_text: Bay Street is named for Upper New York Bay, whose shore it follows from the St. George ferry landing toward the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge. It has been the island's front door since steam ferry days.
history_blurb: Bay Street carries a descriptive name for its route along Upper New York Bay on Staten Island's northeastern shore. The street connected the ferry landings at St. George and Stapleton, villages that grew around Cornelius Vanderbilt's early steam ferry business in the 1810s.

fact_text: Amboy Road is named for Perth Amboy, the New Jersey city it pointed toward via the Tottenville ferry. Amboy itself comes from the Lenape word Ompoge, meaning level ground.
history_blurb: Amboy Road took travelers down the length of Staten Island toward the ferry at Tottenville, which crossed to Perth Amboy, New Jersey. The name Amboy derives from Ompoge, a Lenape word for level or standing ground, one of the oldest place names in the region.

fact_text: Arthur Kill Road is named for the Arthur Kill, the tidal strait it parallels. The name is an anglicization of the Dutch achter kill, meaning back channel, the waterway behind Staten Island.
namesake: The Arthur Kill strait (Dutch achter kill)
history_blurb: Arthur Kill Road follows the Arthur Kill, the tidal strait separating Staten Island from New Jersey. The name has nothing to do with anyone named Arthur. It is an English corruption of the Dutch achter kill, meaning back channel, because the waterway ran behind the island as seen from New Amsterdam.

fact_text: Todt Hill Road climbs the hill whose name comes from a Dutch and German word for death, possibly for the old Moravian Cemetery at its foot or its bare rocky summit. It is the highest natural point on the Atlantic coast south of Maine.
history_blurb: Todt Hill Road is named for Todt Hill, whose name derives from the Dutch and German word for death. Theories point to the Moravian Cemetery in use at its base since colonial days, or to the hill's bare serpentine rock that resisted vegetation. The Dutch once called it Yserberg, or Iron Hill, for its ore. At 401 feet it is the highest natural point on the eastern seaboard south of Maine.

fact_text: Father Capodanno Boulevard honors Vincent Capodanno, a Staten Island born Navy chaplain killed in Vietnam in 1967 while giving last rites under fire. He received the Medal of Honor posthumously.
history_blurb: Father Capodanno Boulevard is named for Vincent Robert Capodanno, a Catholic priest from Staten Island who served as a Navy chaplain with the Marines in Vietnam. He was killed in 1967 while ministering to wounded Marines under enemy fire and was posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor. The former Seaside Boulevard was renamed for him in 1976.

fact_text: New Dorp Lane preserves the Dutch name Nieuw Dorp, meaning new village, the settlement founded around 1671 after the island's original Oude Dorp, or old village, near South Beach.
history_blurb: New Dorp Lane is named for New Dorp, from the Dutch Nieuw Dorp meaning new village. Settlers founded it around 1671 as the successor to Oude Dorp, the old village near South Beach that was Staten Island's first permanent European settlement. The lane was the village's main street to the water.
